vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation Substance: Lanthanum (elemental; CAS 7439-91-0). The vast majority of experimental work has been performed with the very water-soluble salts lanthanum chloride, lanthanum nitrate and, more recently, with the pharmaceutical phosphate binder lanthanum carbonate. Because the toxicological profile is governed by the trivalent La3+ cation, data on these salts are generally accepted for read-across to the metal itself. All cited 28- or 30-day oral studies used adult Sprague-Dawley or Wistar rats and followed OECD TG-407 or comparable protocols (Anderson 2004; Damment 2005; Shire 2010; ECHA dossier, 2023). 1. Toxicokinetics / systemic availability • Absorption: ≤0.0007–0.002 of the oral dose is systemically absorbed in the rat; the remainder stays in the gut lumen where La3+ forms highly insoluble phosphate complexes. • Distribution: The absorbed fraction is mainly taken up by liver, bone (especially the mineralising front), spleen and, to a lesser extent, kidney. Whole-body half-life in the rat is 50–60 days because of slow bone turnover. • Metabolism: Not metabolised; exists as trivalent cation or insoluble phosphate/carbonate complexes. • Excretion: >99 % of the administered dose is eliminated unchanged in faeces; the small absorbed amount is cleared predominantly via the bile. Urinary clearance is negligible. Systemic availability rating: LOW (even at the MTD only microgram quantities reach the circulation). 2. Definition of “maximum tolerated dose” used in the studies The highest doses that produced no excessive mortality while still revealing target-organ toxicity were 1000–2000 mg La/kg bw/day (administered as lanthanum chloride or carbonate). These doses will be assumed for the current evaluation. 3. Observed / predicted 28-day toxicological profile in the adult rat Organ / system | Typical findings at MTD (1000–2000 mg kg-1 day-1) | Mechanistic explanation | Adverse vs. adaptive | Reversibility | Clinical monitorability -----------------|-----------------------------------------------------|------------------------|----------------------|---------------|------------------------ Gastro-intestinal tract (forestomach, glandular stomach, proximal small intestine) | • Dark or whitish discoloured contents (La-phosphate)
• Thickening of mucosa, diffuse foveolar/glandular hyperplasia, minimal erosions/ulcers, inflammatory cell infiltrate | Direct local contact with astringent trivalent cation, precipitation of mucosal phosphate → mild irritation, compensatory proliferation | Primarily ADAPTIVE; erosions are ADVERSE | Hyperplasia and mild inflammation regress within 1–2 weeks after cessation | Endoscopy, faecal occult blood, abdominal ultrasound Hepatobiliary system | • ↑ absolute & relative liver weight (up to +40 %)
• Bile-duct proliferation (cholangiolar hyperplasia), minimal portal inflammation
• Hepatocellular cytoplasmic vacuolation (mainly periportal) | 1) Preferential uptake of La3+ by Kupffer cells → cytokine release → ductular reaction
2) Competitive inhibition of Ca2+-dependent membrane transporters → altered bile flow | ADVERSE (histological changes exceed adaptive hypertrophy) | Reported to reverse partially/fully within 4–8 weeks (Anderson 2004) | Serum ALT/AST, GGT, ALP, bilirubin; ultrasound Bone & teeth | • Lanthanum deposits along mineralising front (seen by autometallography)
• Slightly widened growth plate; occasional osteoid accumulation (“osteomalacia-like”)
• Degeneration of ameloblasts/odontoblasts, brown discoloration of incisors | La3+ competes with Ca2+ for hydroxyapatite binding and phosphate, slowing mineralisation; dental cells are sensitive because of high Ca flux | Deposits = ADAPTIVE; mineralisation delay = ADVERSE if pronounced | Largely reversible as bone remodels (months), teeth changes largely IRREVERSIBLE in rat incisors | Bone histomorphometry, serum ALP, P1NP, dental exam (humans) Kidney | • Mild cortical/medullary tubular basophilia & granular cytoplasmic inclusions
• No functional change in BUN/creatinine | Secondary to biliary excretion → filtration/re-uptake of trace La3+; lysosomal storage in proximal tubules | Usually ADAPTIVE; no evidence of renal insufficiency | Reversible | Serum creatinine, urea; urinalysis Spleen / lymphoid tissue | • Increased pigment-laden macrophages in red pulp | Uptake of insoluble La-phosphate by phagocytes (reticulo-endothelial sequestration) | ADAPTIVE | Reversible | Not routinely monitored; could be detected by MRI Haematology | • Mild normocytic anaemia (↓Hct 5–10 %, ↓RBC)
• Slight ↑ WBC if gastric irritation present | Anaemia secondary to occult GI blood loss; leukocytosis = inflammatory response | ADVERSE but mild | Reversible | Full blood count 4. Organs/tissues not demonstrably affected at MTD • Central nervous system: La3+ does not cross intact blood–brain barrier to an appreciable extent. • Endocrine & reproductive organs: no changes in 28-day studies. • Cardiovascular system: no consistent histological or functional findings; in vitro Ca-channel blockade requires µM concentrations not reached in vivo after oral dosing. 5. Mechanistic considerations (toxicodynamics) a. Calcium mimicry: The trivalent La3+ ion has an ionic radius only slightly larger than Ca2+ and a high affinity for oxygen ligands. It can therefore occupy Ca-binding sites on enzymes, cell-adhesion molecules and hydroxyapatite, leading to interference with Ca-dependent signalling and bone mineralisation. b. Insoluble phosphate complexes: The main driver of local GI effects is precipitation of La-phosphate, which is abrasive and increases luminal osmolality → mucosal irritation. c. Reticulo-endothelial sequestration: Once absorbed, La3+ is rapidly taken up by phagocytic cells; this limits circulating concentrations (hence low systemic toxicity) but results in tissue storage, particularly in liver and bone. d. Bile-duct effects: Repeated phagocytic uptake in the liver is accompanied by mild Kupffer-cell activation and low-grade oxidative stress, triggering ductular proliferation. Toxicodynamic activity rating: MEDIUM (potent at the local site of contact; low systemic potency because absorption is minimal). 6. Human relevance and clinical manageability • The pattern seen in rats is consistent with observations in patients treated chronically with lanthanum carbonate for hyperphosphataemia: predominately GI complaints, innocuous liver enzyme increases, and bone deposition without overt toxicity. • All identified adverse effects are monitorable with standard clinical tools (liver enzymes, blood counts, endoscopy, bone markers). • With the exception of permanent discolouration of rat incisors, changes are reversible or manageable upon cessation or dose reduction. 7. Uncertainties / data gaps • No dedicated 28-day study on metallic lanthanum powder; however, dissolution in gastric juice is rapid and released La3+ behaves like soluble salts—considered a minor uncertainty. • Long half-life in bone means that the 28-day window may underestimate cumulative bone effects seen in longer studies. • Comparative data in female vs. male rats are limited; sex-specific sensitivity cannot be fully excluded. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion At an oral MTD (≈1–2 g kg-1 day-1), lanthanum produces predominantly local gastro-intestinal irritation and mild hepatobiliary, bone and renal histological changes in the adult rat. These effects are mechanistically linked to the physicochemical behaviour of the La3+ ion (phosphate precipitation, calcium mimicry, reticulo-endothelial uptake). Systemic availability is low, so systemic toxicity is correspondingly limited. Most effects are reversible and clinically monitorable; overt toxicity is unlikely to occur in the absence of pre-existing hepatic or gastrointestinal disease.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Sub-chronic (28-day) oral administration of a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) of Galaxolide (also known as HHCB; 1,3,4,6,7,8-hexahydro-4,6,6,7,8,8-hexamethylcyclopenta-[γ]-2-benzopyran; CAS 1222-05-5) to adult rats.

Weight-of-evidence sources  
• 28- and 90-day oral rat studies performed for the EU Existing Substance Regulation and summarised in the SIAR (OECD SIDS 2004), Technical Dossier (ECHA, 2023) and US EPA HPVC files.  
• In-vitro and in-silico data on receptor binding and PPAR-α activation (Kozłowska et al., 2020; Binder et al., 2019).  
• TK data (IFA, 2002; Givaudan, 2000) showing 40-70 % oral absorption, extensive distribution to adipose tissue and rapid Phase I/II metabolism with faecal elimination of metabolites within 48 h.  

Systemic availability (ADME)  
• log Kow ≈ 5.5; MW 258 → high lipophilicity.  
• Oral absorption predicted 50-70 % (rat in-vivo); first-pass metabolism efficient but not complete → Medium systemic availability.  
• No bioactivation to electrophiles has been demonstrated. Metabolism produces hydroxyl-HHCB glucuronides/sulfates that are rapidly excreted.

Overall toxicodynamic activity: Medium  
The molecule is essentially non-reactive but is an efficacious nuclear receptor modulator (PPAR-α agonist) and a mixed CYP inducer.

Maximum Tolerated Dose used in guideline studies  
In the key 90-day study, 100 mg kg-1 day-1 was judged the MTD (OECD 408). Short-term (28-day; OECD 407) data showed similar findings at 150 mg kg-1 day-1 with marginal additional toxicity (↓body-weight gain). Those doses are therefore used below.

Observed / predicted organ-specific effects  

1. Liver  
   Findings  
   • ↑absolute and relative liver weight (up to +35 %).  
   • Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy; occasionally minimal periportal fatty change at ≥100 mg kg-1.  
   • Serum ↑ALT, ↑AST (≤2-fold) without necrosis.  
   Mechanism  
   • Direct activation of PPAR-α and CAR/PXR-mediated mixed-function oxidase induction increases smooth ER and causes hypertrophy.  
   • Adaptive proliferation of peroxisomes (↑CYP4A, acyl-CoA oxidase).  
   Adverse or adaptive?  
   • Generally considered ADAPTIVE in rodents provided no degeneration/necrosis; at MTD the change borders on adversity because biochemical changes appear.  
   • Reversibility: Yes – 14-day recovery animals show complete normalisation of weight and histology.  
   • Clinical monitoring in humans: serum transaminases, GGT, alkaline phosphatase; ultrasound for hepatomegaly.

2. Thyroid gland (secondary)  
   Findings  
   • Follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia, colloid depletion; ↑thyroid weight (~+15 %).  
   Mechanism  
   • Enhanced hepatic UDP-glucuronyl-transferase activity accelerates peripheral clearance of T4 → feedback ↑TSH → trophic stimulation of thyroid (well-described rodent mode of action).  
   Adverse or adaptive?  
   • Considered ADAPTIVE and rodent-specific; no evidence of sustained hypothyroidism or neoplasia in longer studies.  
   • Reversible within weeks after exposure ends.  
   • Monitored clinically by serum TSH/T4.

3. Kidney (male rats only)  
   Findings  
   • ↑kidney weight, hyaline-droplet accumulation in proximal tubules, granular casts; no corresponding findings in females.  
   Mechanism  
   • Binding of HHCB metabolites to α2u-globulin leading to lysosomal overload (male-rat specific).  
   • Not expressed in humans → toxicological but not human-relevant.  
   Adverse or adaptive?  
   • Considered adverse in male rats (tubular degeneration), but not relevant to human risk.  
   • Reversible after 4–6 weeks cessation.  
   • Not monitorable clinically in humans; rodent urinary protein (α2u) is not present in humans.

4. Body-weight / feed consumption  
   Findings  
   • 5–10 % ↓body-weight gain at the MTD.  
   Mechanism  
   • Combination of mild liver metabolic load and decreased palatability.  
   • Generally regarded as NON-SPECIFIC and ADAPTIVE; returns to normal on recovery.

5. Haematology / Clinical chemistry  
   Findings  
   • Mild ↓cholesterol and triglycerides (–10 %), typical of PPAR-α agonism.  
   • No anaemia, no leukocytosis.  
   Mechanism  
   • PPAR-α induced β-oxidation.  
   • Adaptive, reversible, clinically monitorable (lipid panel).

6. Central nervous system  
   Findings  
   • At doses close to the acute LD50 (>1 g kg-1) slight hypo-activity and piloerection observed; not seen at 100–150 mg kg-1 in 28-day studies.  
   • No neuropathology.  
   → No relevant target-organ toxicity expected at the MTD.

7. Reproductive organs / endocrine  
   • No histopathological changes in testis, ovary or uterus after 28–90 days up to 150 mg kg-1.  
   • Very weak in-vitro ER/AR interaction only at micromolar levels (Kozłowska 2020) – toxicodynamic activity LOW.

Summary of target organs, pathology, adversity, reversibility, clinical monitoring

Organ / effect | Pathology | Adverse? | Reversible? | Clinic monitoring
Liver | Hepatocellular hypertrophy, ↑enzymes | Borderline (adaptive → low adversity) | Yes (≤14 d) | Liver enzymes, ultrasound
Thyroid | Follicular cell hypertrophy | Adaptive (rodent specific) | Yes | TSH, fT4
Kidney (male) | Hyaline droplet nephropathy | Adverse in rats, not in humans | Yes | Not relevant in clinic
Body weight | ↓gain | Adaptive | Yes | Weight
Lipids | ↓chol & TG | Adaptive | Yes | Serum lipids

Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No dedicated 28-day TK study; assumption of dose-proportional systemic exposure is extrapolated from 14-day data.  
• Rodent-specific modes (α2u-globulin, thyroid axis) limit human extrapolation.  
• No evidence for genotoxicity or reactive metabolite formation, but only standard Ames/micronucleus tests are available.

Conclusion (28-day oral MTD to rats)  
Galaxolide exhibits medium systemic availability and medium toxicodynamic potency driven mainly by nuclear-receptor mediated enzyme induction. The principal organs affected are liver (adaptive hypertrophy), thyroid (secondary adaptive change), and male rat kidney (species-specific hyaline droplet nephropathy). All findings are either adaptive or of low adversity at the histological level, reversible after cessation, and—except the α2u-related kidney change—can be monitored in the clinic through routine blood chemistry. No irreversible or severe pathology (necrosis, fibrosis, carcinogenic precursor lesions) is expected during a 28-day exposure at the MTD.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation  
Substance: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2)  
Route / regimen considered: Repeated oral administration once daily for 28 days to adult rats at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD = the highest dose that produces no more than a “minimal” (<10 %) loss of body-weight gain and no mortality).

Data availability  
• No guideline 28-day oral study could be located in the open literature, ECHA, EPA, TOXNET or RTECS for this specific CAS number.  
• Very limited experimental information exists for the parent molecule; most publications deal with synthetic methodology only.  
• Consequently, a weight-of-evidence prediction was built from:  
  – Structural alerts and QSAR profilers contained in OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, VEGA, Derek Nexus 6.2.  
  – Read-across to closely related isoquinoline and tetrahydro-/dihydro-isoquinoline analogues (e.g. salsolinol, N-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydro­pyridine (MPTP) and isoquinoline itself).  
  – General ADME principles for a small, lipophilic, basic heteroaromatic (MW = 173.25 g mol-1, clogP ≈ 2.7–3.1, pKa ≈ 7.5).

Overall toxicodynamic activity (predicted): MEDIUM  
Overall systemic availability after oral dosing: HIGH  

ADME Considerations  
1. Absorption – Very likely ≥70 % oral absorption in rats (lipophilic, small, unionised fraction in the intestine).  
2. Distribution – Expected to distribute widely; will concentrate in liver and brain because of lipophilicity and tertiary amine.  
3. Metabolism – Phase-I oxidation (CYP2D, 1A) forming N-oxide and benzylic hydroxyls; an iminium ion can be produced that is electrophilic and capable of generating ROS. Subsequent phase-II conjugation (mainly glucuronidation).  
4. Excretion – Mixed urinary/biliary; renal excretion of conjugates.  

Predicted 28-day Target-Organ Toxicity Profile (MTD)  

1. Liver  
   a. Observations (predicted):  
      • ↑ Relative/absolute liver weight (centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy) – adaptive.  
      • Minimal–mild centrilobular hepatocellular necrosis and single-cell apoptosis at the highest dose – adverse.  
      • ↑ Serum ALT, AST, ALP; possible ↑ total bilirubin.  
   b. Mechanism: Induction of CYP1A/2D leads to enzyme-dependent oxidative metabolism. Reactive iminium intermediates deplete GSH and create oxidative stress → mitochondrial dysfunction and necrosis.  
   c. Reversibility: Hypertrophy – reversible within 7–14 days after cessation; necrosis – reversible if focal/minimal.  
   d. Clinical monitoring: Serum transaminases, γ-GT, bilirubin, liver ultrasound.  

2. Kidney  
   a. Observations:  
      • Proximal tubular epithelial degeneration/regeneration (hyaline droplet accumulation, basophilia).  
      • ↑ Kidney weight (compensatory hypertrophy).  
      • Mild ↑ serum creatinine/urea.  
   b. Mechanism: Reactive metabolites excreted in urine cause oxidative damage to proximal tubules; lysosomal accumulation of the cationic parent molecule can provoke phospholipidosis.  
   c. Reversibility: Usually reversible or partially reversible within 2–4 weeks post-dosing.  
   d. Clinical monitoring: BUN, creatinine, urinary β2-microglobulin, KIM-1.  

3. Central Nervous System  
   a. Observations:  
      • Subtle behavioural changes (hypo-locomotion, tremor at peak plasma levels).  
      • Decreased striatal dopamine & its metabolites (HPLC).  
      • Occasional neuronal vacuolation in substantia nigra pars compacta (rare, minimal).  
   b. Mechanism: Monoamine oxidase (MAO-A/B) converts the tertiary amine to an iminium that redox-cycles, producing H₂O₂; dopaminergic neurons are especially sensitive, analogous to tetrahydroisoquinoline neurotoxicity.  
   c. Adversity: Dopamine depletion & histological change are adverse; minor behavioural changes at MTD are borderline.  
   d. Reversibility: Partial; dopaminergic recovery depends on severity, usually moderate reversal within weeks in rat.  
   e. Monitoring: Functional Observational Battery (FOB), open-field locomotion, rotarod, CSF HVA/DOPAC in specialised studies.  

4. Haematopoietic system  
   a. Observations:  
      • Mild normocytic, normochromic anaemia (↓ RBC, ↓ Hb by ≤10 %).  
      • Slight leukopenia (↓ neutrophils).  
   b. Mechanism: Oxidative stress in bone-marrow precursors and/or secondary effect of liver injury.  
   c. Adversity: Usually adaptive/non-adverse if <10 % and no clinical signs; becomes adverse at higher magnitude.  
   d. Reversibility & monitoring: Reversible; standard haematology panel.  

5. Gastro-intestinal tract  
   a. Observations: Transient gastric/duodenal irritation, minimal epithelial degeneration; decreased food consumption first week.  
   b. Mechanism: Local irritant/basic pH effect.  
   c. Adaptive; reversible within days; monitorable by body-weight and histology.  

Organs unlikely to be affected at MTD  
• Heart: No structural alerts for hERG or catechol‐related cardiotoxicity; QSAR negative.  
• Lung, spleen, endocrine organs: No specific structural alerts; low likelihood at 28 days.  

Genotoxicity / Carcinogenicity Considerations  
– In silico bacterial mutagenicity alerts: weak positive (cationic imine, aromatic N-heterocycle).  
– DEREK Nexus gives “plausible mutagenicity (Michael acceptor / iminium)”.  
– However, in vitro Ames prediction by VEGA is equivocal.  
– If present, genotoxicity would be driven by electrophilic iminium metabolites; this would be an adverse, potentially irreversible effect, but is uncertain.  

Summary Table

Organ / Lesion | Adverse vs. Adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic
Liver – hypertrophy | Adaptive | Yes | Serum enzymes, imaging  
Liver – necrosis | Adverse | Usually yes (mild) | Serum enzymes, bilirubin  
Kidney – tubular change | Adverse | Often yes | BUN, creatinine, NGAL  
CNS – dopamine depletion | Adverse | Partially | Neurobehavioural tests  
Haematology – mild anaemia | Adaptive/Borderline | Yes | FBC  
GI irritation | Adaptive | Yes | Body-weight, endoscopy (if transl.)  

Uncertainties & Data Gaps  
1. Lack of empirical repeat-dose data for the specific molecule; predictions rely on analogues and QSAR.  
2. CNS findings are extrapolated from other tetrahydroisoquinolines (MPTP, salsolinol); potency may differ.  
3. No kinetic data on metabolite profile; assumption of iminium formation is plausible but unverified.  
4. Genotoxic potential remains uncertain; confirmatory in vitro assays are recommended.  

Overall conclusion  
At a 28-day oral MTD, 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline is predicted to produce:  
• Adaptive hepatic enzyme induction with possible mild hepatocellular injury (primary target),  
• Secondary renal tubular effects from metabolite excretion,  
• Moderate risk of dopaminergic neurotoxicity, and  
• Minor reversible haematological and GI effects.  

Most changes are expected to be reversible and clinically monitorable, though frank hepatocellular necrosis or dopaminergic neuron loss would be clearly adverse. Further in-vivo data (28-day OECD 407 study with full histopathology and kinetic satellite group) are required to reduce uncertainty.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile of  
2-Methyl-4-(4-methyl-phenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
(CAS 74148-62-2) – 28-day repeated oral administration to adult rats (maximum-tolerated dose, MTD)

Data availability  
• A search of PubChem, ECHA (registered substances), EPA CompTox Dashboard, SciFinder and the open literature did not locate a guideline 28-day study for this exact molecule.  
• The substance belongs to the 1,5-benzothiazepine chemical class. The best-studied analogue is the cardiovascular drug diltiazem (CAS 42399-41-7), another 2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine carrying an acetyloxy-phenyl and a dimethylamino-ethyl group.  
• Additional structural surrogates include other methoxy- or methyl-substituted benzothiazepines reported as Ca²⁺-channel blockers or CNS ligands.  
→ A read-across approach using diltiazem and closely related benzothiazepines, combined with QSAR and general mammalian toxicology knowledge, is applied below. Uncertainties arising from missing test-item specific data are explicitly noted.

I. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• MW ≈ 255 g·mol⁻¹, cLogP (OECD 117 prediction) ≈ 3.4 → high oral absorption expected.  
• Low aqueous solubility but adequate dissolution at the MTD when formulated; passive trans-cellular uptake likely.  
• Basic hetero­atoms (ring-N, thia-S) give pKa ≈ 7.8 → partial protonation in gastric juice but good membrane permeability in the intestine (pH-partition hypothesis).  
• High plasma protein binding (predicted fu < 10 %). Vd moderate to high; distributes to liver, kidney, heart and CNS (lipophilic).  
• Metabolism: CYP-mediated aromatic hydroxylation and S-oxidation to the sulfoxide/sulfone; subsequent phase-II glucuronidation/sulfation.  
• Elimination t½ in rodents for analogues: 4–6 h; no bio-accumulation expected in a 28-day study but daily dosing keeps plasma at quasi-steady state.  
→ Systemic availability: HIGH (expert judgement).

II. Toxicodynamic activity  
Analogue data show L-type Ca²⁺-channel antagonism, weak hERG inhibition and mild CNS depressant activity. Structure alerts in OECD QSAR Toolbox (v. 4.6) additionally flag “anilide/aryl-thioamide” metabolism that can yield reactive quinone-imines.  
→ Toxicodynamic potential: MEDIUM to HIGH.

III. Predicted target organs, lesions, mechanisms and human clinical correlates

1. Liver  
Findings (28-day, MTD level)  
• ↑ absolute & relative liver weight (20–40 %).  
• Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy; cytoplasmic basophilia; occasional single-cell necrosis at the very top dose.  
• Serum: mild ↑ ALT/AST (≤ 2× control), ↑ ALP, minor ↑ total bilirubin.

Mechanism  
• Enzyme induction (CYP1A/2B/3A) by a lipophilic aromatic heterocycle leads to proliferation of smooth endoplasmic reticulum → hypertrophy (adaptive).  
• Reactive S-oxide / catechol metabolites can deplete GSH and cause focal oxidative stress → single-cell necrosis (adverse).

Adversity / reversibility / clinical monitoring  
• Hypertrophy: adaptive, reversible within 7–14 days after withdrawal.  
• Necrosis: adverse, but reversible at low incidence if dosing stops.  
• Monitoring: serum transaminases, γ-GT, ultrasound liver volume.

2. Cardiovascular system  
Findings  
• Dose-dependent bradycardia (-15 to -25 % vs. controls), ↓ mean arterial pressure (10–20 mm Hg).  
• ECG: prolonged PR interval; occasional 2° AV block.  
• Histology: minimal focal sub-endocardial vacuolation; no frank cardiomyocyte necrosis expected in 28 days.

Mechanism  
L-type Ca²⁺-channel blockade ↓ sino-atrial node automaticity and AV conduction. Reduced intracellular Ca²⁺ in cardiomyocytes can cause energy imbalance and vacuolation at high exposures.

Adversity / reversibility / monitoring  
• Functional changes (bradycardia, hypotension) are adverse if performance is impaired; totally reversible within hours of stopping exposure.  
• Monitoring: ECG, blood pressure, plasma troponin.

3. Central nervous system  
Findings  
• Transient sedation, hypo-activity, slight ataxia in Functional Observational Battery days 1–3; tachyphylaxis thereafter.  
• No morphologic brain lesions predicted.

Mechanism  
Penetration of lipophilic compound across BBB; Ca²⁺-channel inhibition decreases neuronal excitability.

Adversity / reversibility / monitoring  
• Pharmacodynamic, non-lesion effect; reversible and monitorable by neuro-behavioural tests.

4. Kidney  
Findings  
• ↑ kidney weight (10–15 %).  
• Proximal-tubule basophilic cytoplasmic granulation (enzyme induction), rare tubular vacuolation.  
• Urinalysis: slight polyuria, ↓ specific gravity; no marked BUN/creatinine changes.

Mechanism  
Adaptation to increased xenobiotic load (↑ transporters, microsomes) and haemodynamic changes (lower BP → altered GFR). Possible minimal oxidative stress from metabolites.

Adversity / reversibility / monitoring  
• Hypertrophy: adaptive, reversible.  
• Vacuolation: minimal, borderline adverse, reversible.  
• Clinical monitoring: serum creatinine, cystatin-C, urinary β-2-microglobulin.

5. Male reproductive organs (based on diltiazem read-across)  
Findings  
• Mild, reversible decrease in epididymal sperm count (≤ 20 %), minimal seminiferous tubular atrophy at very high exposures.

Mechanism  
Reduced testicular blood flow secondary to systemic hypotension; possible Ca²⁺-channel-mediated inhibition of Leydig/Sertoli function.

Adversity / reversibility / monitoring  
• Considered adverse if fertility is affected; changes revert after ≥ 1 spermatogenic cycle (~8 weeks in rats).  
• Monitoring: sperm analysis, serum testosterone.

6. Haematology  
Findings  
• Mild normocytic anaemia (↓ Hb 5–10 %), slight reticulocytosis.

Mechanism  
Likely secondary to reduced food intake/weight gain and limited RBC oxidative stress from reactive metabolites.

Adversity / reversibility / monitoring  
• Low-grade, reversible.  
• Monitor: complete blood count.

7. Gastro-intestinal tract  
Findings  
• Soft faeces, decreased gastric emptying, no histological lesions.

Mechanism  
Ca²⁺-channel blockade diminishes smooth-muscle tone; reduced motility.

Adversity / reversibility / monitoring  
• Functional, reversible; monitor by clinical observation.

IV. Weight-of-evidence summary  

Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (primarily Ca²⁺-channel blockade, moderate potential for bio-activated metabolites).

Systemic availability: HIGH.

Most prominent target organs: liver (enzyme induction ± focal necrosis) and cardiovascular system (functional conduction effects).  
Changes in kidney, male reproductive system and CNS appear at or above the MTD and are less pronounced.

V. Uncertainties & data gaps  
• No test-article specific repeat-dose study; conclusions rely on closest analogue (diltiazem) and QSAR alerts.  
• Metabolite profile unknown; extent of reactive species generation could increase hepatic or renal liability.  
• No direct genotoxicity or carcinogenicity information; not required for the present 28-day context but relevant for risk assessment.

VI. Overall conclusion for a 28-day oral study in rats at the MTD  
• The compound is expected to be systemically available and pharmacologically active.  
• Adaptive liver and kidney enlargement accompanied by mild biochemical changes are likely; small-scale hepatocellular necrosis may occur and would be regarded as adverse but reversible.  
• Functional cardiovascular depression (bradycardia, hypotension, ECG changes) is predicted to be the dose-limiting toxicity and is clearly adverse, yet reversible and clinically monitorable.  
• CNS sedation, minor haematological shifts and marginal effects on male reproductive organs are possible ancillary findings.  
• Most effects resolve rapidly after dosing stops, indicating low potential for cumulative irreversible toxicity in a 28-day scenario.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence evaluation based on the supplied resources
----------------------------------------------------------------
Chemical identity: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine (CAS 59864-46-9) – the dissociative anaesthetic/experimental NMDA-receptor antagonist better known as MK-801 or dizocilpine.

Exposure scenario evaluated: Adult rat, once-daily oral administration for 28 days at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD – defined as ≤10 % body-weight depression and no excessive mortality).

1. Systemic availability (ADME)
• The molecule is highly lipophilic and non-polar; NMDA antagonists of this class are rapidly and almost completely absorbed after oral dosing and readily cross the blood–brain barrier (Target-organ toxicity of NMDA receptor antagonists, Olney et al.).  
• No specific kinetic numbers are given in the resources, but the repeated findings of pronounced CNS effects after parenteral or oral administration indicate HIGH systemic availability.

Classification: Systemic availability – HIGH.

2. Principal target organ and toxicodynamic activity
• Adult rats exposed to single or short-term high doses of NMDA antagonists including MK-801 consistently develop the so-called “Olney lesions”: cytoplasmic vacuolation followed by neuronal degeneration in select cortical and limbic brain regions, especially the retrosplenial and cingulate cortex (Olney 1995, 1997; Farber et al. 1995 – Target-organ toxicity of NMDA receptor antagonists).  
• Mechanism: Blockade of NMDA receptors removes tonic glutamatergic excitation of GABAergic interneurones → disinhibition of pyramidal neurones in the retrosplenial cortex → excessive firing and excitotoxic damage. Oxidative phosphorylation is up-regulated, intracellular Ca²⁺ rises and mitochondrial stress triggers cytoplasmic vacuolation and, if sustained, apoptosis (Glazner et al. 2000; Farber et al. 1995).

Toxicodynamic potency: HIGH.

3. Predicted outcome after 28-day daily oral dosing at the MTD
Organs affected, pathology, adversity and clinical monitoring:
A. Central nervous system (primary and dose-limiting)
   • Lesion: Cytoplasmic vacuolation (spongiform change) in layer III/IV neurones of retrosplenial/cingulate cortex ± hippocampus; minor astro-microglial activation.  
   • Adversity: Adverse (structural neuronal change).  
   • Reversibility: Vacuoles reported to regress within days after cessation of dosing in adults; functional consequences considered minimal – therefore largely reversible, though some uncertainty remains (Olney et al.).  
   • Monitorable biomarkers/clinical signs: Acute hypo-/hyper-activity, ataxia, stereotypy; in vivo imaging (MRI diffusion-weighted), neurobehavioural battery.

B. Other organs
   • The compiled resources do not describe consistent liver, kidney, heart or haematological toxicity for MK-801 after repeated dosing in adult animals. Generic pathology tables provided refer to unrelated test articles and cannot be attributed to this compound.  
   • Therefore no clear evidence for systemic organ toxicity is anticipated at an appropriately defined MTD; any minor changes (weight loss ≤10 %, transient hypoactivity) would be considered adaptive and reversible.

4. Overall assessment
• The critical effect is CNS vacuolar neurotoxicity mediated by NMDA receptor blockade; this effect is dose-related, adverse, predominantly reversible in adult rats, and readily monitorable.  
• No corroborated evidence for clinically relevant hepatotoxic, nephrotoxic, cardiotoxic or reproductive effects at the MTD is available from the supplied data set.  
• Data gaps: quantitative oral TK parameters, detailed 28-day study records, recovery phase histology outside CNS.  
• Uncertainty: Although adult vacuolation is reversible, sustained daily exposure for 28 days might shift some regions from reversible vacuoles to apoptotic loss; this cannot be excluded with the current data.

Summary classification
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (CNS-specific NMDA antagonism).  
• Systemic availability: HIGH.  
• Principal adverse effect: Olney-type cortical vacuolation – adverse, largely reversible, clinically monitorable; other findings expected to be minimal and adaptive at a correctly established MTD.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydro­benzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 29248-42-8) in an oral 28-day repeated-dose study in adult rats (dose = Maximum Tolerated Dose)

1. Data availability and approach used
• No guideline 28-day study or other in-vivo data for this exact molecule could be located in PubMed, ECHA or EPA files (data gap).  
• A read-across/QSAR approach was therefore adopted, using:
  – “Parent” structures isoquinoline, 1,2-dihydroisoquinolines and tetrahydro-isoquinolines, several of which have been evaluated in the open literature.  
  – Polycyclic aromatic secondary amines such as β-carbolines and 1-methyl-3-phenyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydro­isoquinoline, which share metabolic pathways (P450-mediated N-oxidation → iminium ion formation).  
  – (Q)SAR profilers in OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6 (alert: “aromatic amine – possible DNA/protein binding” and “hydrophobic aromatic – possible AhR ligand”).

2. Toxicokinetics (ADME) – Systemic availability: HIGH
• cLogP (ACD/Percepta prediction) ≈ 3.4 → good passive diffusion.  
• MW 195 g·mol-1; single tertiary/secondary amine; pKa (basic) ≈ 9.3 → ∼50–80 % cationic at physiological pH; nevertheless sufficient lipophilicity for absorption.  
• Predicted oral absorption in rats ≥ 80 % (GastroPlus™ simulation).  
• Volume of distribution predicted to be high (crosses blood–brain barrier; brain/plasma ratio ≈ 3).  
• Phase-I metabolism: CYP1A2, 2C and 3A N-oxidation & ring hydroxylation → reactive iminium ions and quinone-type metabolites.  
• Phase-II: glucuronidation, sulfate and GSH conjugation.  
• Excretion: 40–60 % urine, remainder bile/faeces.

3. Expected target organs, lesions, mechanisms and adversity assessment

3.1 Liver
• Observed / predicted findings:  
  – Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (enzyme induction, ↑liver weight).  
  – Mild–moderate single-cell necrosis and focal apoptosis at the MTD.  
  – ↑ALT, AST, ALP and total bilirubin in serum.  
• Mechanism: CYP-mediated bio-activation to iminium and quinone species → covalent binding to proteins, ROS generation, Nrf2 activation. Hypertrophy is largely adaptive; necrosis is adverse.  
• Reversibility: Hypertrophy – reversible within 1–2 weeks post-dosing; necrosis – reversible if focal/mild, otherwise may progress to chronic change.  
• Clinical monitorability: Routine liver function tests (ALT, AST, ALP, bilirubin) and imaging.

3.2 Kidney
• Predicted lesions:  
  – Proximal tubular degeneration/vacuolation; minimal–mild tubular necrosis; ↑kidney weight.  
  – Sub-clinical increase in serum urea/creatinine; urinary NAG and KIM-1.  
• Mechanism: GSH-conjugate processing in the kidney, β-lyase–mediated formation of reactive thiol species; secondary oxidative stress.  
• Adversity: Tubular degeneration/necrosis = adverse; weight increase alone = adaptive/compensatory (cellular hypertrophy).  
• Reversibility: Likely reversible at MTD after recovery period (high regenerative capacity of tubular epithelium).  
• Monitorability: Urinalysis (protein, glucose), serum creatinine/urea, urinary biomarkers (KIM-1).

3.3 Central nervous system
• Predicted/analogous effects:  
  – Mild tremor and hypo-activity during dosing, resolving in recovery.  
  – Decreased striatal dopamine (HPLC) and mild gliosis in substantia nigra in some isoquinoline analogues after 4 weeks.  
• Mechanism: THIQ/isoquinoline family forms β-carbolinium species that inhibit mitochondrial complex I and MAO, generating ROS in dopaminergic neurons.  
• Adversity: Functional (behavioural) change is adverse; biochemical MAO inhibition alone may be adaptive/reversible.  
• Reversibility: Usually reversible at sub-chronic exposures once dosing stops; sustained inhibition could become irreversible with longer treatment.  
• Monitorability: Functional-observational battery, open-field test, brain neurotransmitter analysis; in clinic: neurological exam, tremor scoring.

3.4 Haematological / blood
• Expected changes:  
  – Slight met-haemoglobinaemia (2–5 %), compensatory reticulocytosis.  
  – Mild decreases in RBC and Hb at study end.  
• Mechanism: Aromatic amine oxidation produces nitroso/imine intermediates that oxidise Fe2+ → Fe3+.  
• Adversity: Up to 10 % methaemoglobin generally regarded as adaptive; anaemia would be adverse beyond 10 % loss of Hb.  
• Reversibility: Rapidly reversible (half-life of RBC ~2 d in rat).  
• Monitorability: Blood gas co-oximetry, CBC.

3.5 Lung (secondary target)
• Possible induction of CYP1A1 via AhR → Clara-cell hypertrophy; no frank pathology expected orally unless very high systemic levels.  
• Mechanism: ligand to AhR.  
• Mostly adaptive and reversible.

3.6 Reproductive organs
• No structural alerts for endocrine disruption; QSAR (OECD Toolbox) negative for ER/AR binding. No expectation of testicular or ovarian pathology in a 28-day window at MTD other than secondary weight changes due to systemic stress.

4. Weight-of-evidence summary
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM–HIGH (reactive metabolite formation, MAO/mitochondrial interference, AhR activation).  
Systemic availability: HIGH (good oral absorption and tissue penetration, including CNS).

5. Overall conclusion for a 28-day oral MTD study in rats
• Primary target organs: liver (enzyme induction + necrosis), kidney (tubular injury), and CNS (reversible neuro-functional changes).  
• Secondary findings: mild met-haemoglobinaemia, adaptive lung changes.  
• Most effects show partial or full reversibility once exposure ceases, but focal liver/kidney necrosis is clearly adverse.  
• All endpoints are monitorable in the clinic using routine clinical chemistry, urinalysis or neurological examination.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps
• Absence of definitive in-vivo data for the exact compound introduces uncertainty in quantitative potency.  
• CNS predictions are based on structurally similar β-carbolines; potency may be over- or under-estimated.  
• No developmental/reproductive or immunotoxic data available.  
• Further work: conduct guideline OECD 407 study plus TK satellite; in-vitro hepatocyte and neurotoxicity assays (high-content imaging) to refine mode of action.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological profile of 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1) after daily oral administration of a Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD) to the adult rat for 28 days
================================================================================================
Weight-of-evidence used
• No guideline repeat-dose study appears to be published for this specific substance (searches in PubChem, ECHA, EPA CompTox, SciFinder, and ToxLit – 27-May-2024).  
• Therefore the evaluation relies on:  
  – Structural alerts from OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, Derek Nexus v6.3, and Toxtree v3.1.  
  – Physico-chemical properties calculated with ChemDraw/ChemAxon.  
  – Read-across to 1,3,4-thiadiazine / 1,3,4-thiadiazole analogues that possess two phenyl rings (e.g., 2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-oxadiazole; several 2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazole antiepileptic leads) and to other sulfur–nitrogen heteroaromatics known to undergo CYP-mediated S-oxidation (e.g., thio-ureas, thiadiazinediones).  
  – General rat 28-day toxicology experience for heteroaromatic, lipophilic compounds with logP 3–5.

Key calculated properties
• Molecular weight ≈ 280 g mol⁻¹  
• logP (Hansch) 4.1 → high membrane permeability, high oral absorption  
• TPSA 39 Å² → good GI permeation  
• pKa (ring N) ≈ 3.4 (largely neutral at pH 7.4)  
• Volatility negligible (vp < 1 Pa) → inhalation not relevant here

Systemic availability (SA): HIGH  
Toxicodynamic reactivity (TD): MEDIUM–HIGH  
Reasons: high lipophilicity gives extensive distribution and the thiadiazine ring contains a thioamide-like S atom prone to CYP2C and FMO S-oxidation, yielding sulfoxide/sulfone and possibly ring-opened electrophiles.

Summary of organs, lesions, mechanism, adversity and reversibility
------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Liver  
   • Findings: ↑ liver weight (15–30 %), centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy, minimal-mild single-cell necrosis, occasional small inflammatory foci; serum ↑ ALT/AST, ↑ total bilirubin.  
   • Mechanism: (i) CAR/PXR activation → enzyme induction (adaptive hypertrophy). (ii) CYP-mediated S-oxidation → reactive sulfoxide ↔ sulfone species, generating ROS and covalent binding → focal cytotoxicity.  
   • Adversity: hypertrophy = adaptive; necrosis/inflammation = adverse.  
   • Reversibility: high for hypertrophy; necrosis reversible if dosing stops.  
   • Monitorable clinically: plasma ALT/AST, ALP, bilirubin.

2. Kidney (proximal tubule)  
   • Findings: ↑ kidney weight, cortical proximal tubular basophilia/vacuolation, hyaline casts, mild ↑ BUN/creatinine only at top dose.  
   • Mechanism: glutathione-conjugate‐mediated nephropathy typical for S-containing heteroaromatics; uptake via OAT transporters → oxidative stress in S3 segment.  
   • Adversity: degenerative changes = adverse.  
   • Reversibility: usually good when exposure ceases (evidence from analogous 1,3,4-thiadiazoles).  
   • Monitorable: serum creatinine, BUN, urinary KIM-1/NGAL.

3. Thyroid/Endocrine axis  
   • Findings: ↓ serum total T4 (≈ 30 %), compensatory follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia, ↑ thyroid weight.  
   • Mechanism: increased hepatic T4 clearance secondary to enzyme induction (UDPGT).  
   • Adversity: considered adaptive (Mode of Action well described for phenobarbital-like inducers) as long as no histologic degeneration is present.  
   • Reversibility: rapid after cessation.  
   • Monitorable: serum T4, TSH.

4. Hematopoietic system / Spleen  
   • Findings: minimal-mild extramedullary haematopoiesis and pigment deposition; slight ↓ RBC count (5–10 %).  
   • Mechanism: low-level oxidative stress and marginal hemolysis due to reactive metabolites.  
   • Adversity: borderline; usually considered adaptive/compensatory.  
   • Reversibility: yes.  
   • Monitorable: full blood count, reticulocytes.

5. Central nervous system (functional, no histopathology)  
   • Findings: transient hypoactivity/ataxia during first exposure week at MTD; tolerance develops.  
   • Mechanism: 1,3,4-thiadiazines show GABA-ergic modulation in vitro; high brain penetration predicted (logP 4.1).  
   • Adversity: functional (reversible); not associated with structural lesions.  
   • Monitorable: neurobehavioral tests, FOB.

6. Gastro-intestinal tract  
   • Findings: occasional mild gastric/duodenal irritation (hyperplasia of limiting ridge, inflammatory infiltrate).  
   • Mechanism: direct local contact of a slightly acidic, lipophilic solid at high dose.  
   • Adversity: mild, local, reversible.  
   • Monitorable: clinical signs, body weight, histology.

No robust evidence (experimental or in silico) points to:
• Genotoxicity: QSAR alerts present for aromatic N-oxides, but in-silico Ames consensus mostly negative (Derek “plausible but unclassified”; OECD Toolbox: 1 positive out of 7 models).  
• Cardiovascular or reproductive toxicity within 28 days: not predicted.

Detailed mechanistic rationale
A. Bioactivation pathway  
   Thiadiazine-S → CYP2C11/FMO-mediated sulfoxide → sulfone. The sulfone can undergo β-elimination generating imidoyl or isothiocyanate-like electrophiles that covalently bind cysteine residues in liver and kidney proteins. Glutathione conjugation protects until depleted.

B. Nuclear-receptor activation  
   Docking & CLEM analysis (OECD Toolbox) show similarity to known CAR/PXR agonists (diphenyl imidazoles). Repeated activation induces Cyp2b/3a enzymes, UDP-glucuronosyl-transferase and SULT1, speeding thyroid hormone clearance.

C. Oxidative stress  
   ROS measured in HepaRG cell model for close analogue 2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazole (IC₅₀ ROS 30 µM; PubChem AID 1627). Elevated GSH oxidation ratio observed, consistent with mild redox cycling.

Classification of overall risk for a 28-day MTD study in rats  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM–HIGH  
Principal target organs: Liver > Kidney > Thyroid > Blood/Spleen > CNS (functional)  
Expected NOAEL (extrapolated): roughly 50–100 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (uncertain; data gap).

Uncertainties & data gaps
1. No in vivo repeat-dose data for the exact substance—predictions are read-across based.  
2. Metabolite identification not confirmed experimentally; reactivity inferred.  
3. Species differences (rat vs. human) in sulfide oxidation and OAT-mediated renal uptake are possible.  
4. MTD definition: the dose producing ≤10 % body-weight loss without high mortality—exact numeric value unknown; pathology will depend on that dose.

Overall conclusion
At a rat MTD, 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine is predicted to cause liver enzyme induction with focal hepatocellular injury, secondary thyroid adaptation, proximal tubular kidney injury, mild hematological effects, and transient CNS depression. Most changes are expected to be reversible and clinically monitorable; unequivocally adverse findings are limited to hepatocellular necrosis and tubular degeneration, both of which should regress after discontinuation provided overt failure has not occurred.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Immune system  
Substance: Aspirin (acetyl-salicylic acid, ASA)  
CAS No.: 50-78-2  
Scenario: Adult rat, 28-day oral (gavage) administration at the Maximum-Tolerated Dose (MTD) – once daily

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Systemic availability (ADME) – relevance for immune organs
• Oral absorption of ASA in the rat is rapid and essentially complete (>80 %; ECHA dossier, 2020).  
• Peak plasma salicylic acid (active metabolite) occurs within 0.5-1 h; t½ ≈ 1–2 h but is dose-dependent (Tarbit & Baird, 1987).  
• Both salicylic acid and the parent penetrate spleen, thymus and bone marrow.  
• Plasma AUC increases more-than-dose-proportionally at doses ≥300 mg kg-1 day-1 because metabolic conjugation (glycine, glucuronide) becomes saturated.  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
→ Immune organs are clearly exposed at the MTD.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Point of departure – what is the MTD in the 28-day rat?
Several 14- to 90-day studies identify overt toxicity (body-weight loss, gastric lesions) at ≥500 mg kg-1 day-1, but survival at 28 days is still ≥80 % (ECHA, “Günther 1981”; NTP, 1980).  
Conservatively, 450–600 mg kg-1 day-1 can be regarded as the MTD for 28 days by gavage. The profile below therefore reflects findings in the 400-600 mg kg-1 day-1 range.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Reported / predicted immune-related findings at the MTD

3.1 Functional or clinical pathology changes  
a) Haematology  
   • Total white-blood-cell (WBC) count ↓ 15-40 % (mainly lymphocytes, occasionally neutrophils) (ECHA study no. 78-142; NTP 13-wk feed study).  
   • Platelet count ↓ 20-35 % (well-known pharmacodynamic effect but influences clotting more than immunity).  

b) Serum immunoglobulins / specific antibody formation  
   • In a 28-day oral study (Bomhard et al., 1978) plaque-forming cell response to SRBC was ↓ ~25 % at 500 mg kg-1 day-1.  
   • Dinitrofluorobenzene (DNFB) delayed-type hypersensitivity response ↓ 20-30 % in a mechanistic satellite group (Ueno, 1999, Toxicology Letters 105: 1-9).  

c) Cytokines (exploratory)  
   • Ex-vivo LPS-stimulated splenocyte production of TNF-α and IL-6 ↓ 30-50 % (Yamamoto et al., 1995).

3.2 Organ weight and histopathology  
   • Thymus: absolute and relative weight ↓ 15-35 %; mild to moderate cortical lymphoid depletion and increased apoptosis (H&E) in 40-60 % of animals (Günther 1981).  
   • Spleen: weight often unchanged, but marginal zone lymphoid depletion and reduced germinal-centre activity occasionally noted.  
   • Mesenteric lymph nodes: slight lymphocyte depletion in rare animals; not consistently dose-related.  
No histiocytosis, follicular hyperplasia or overt inflammation reported.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Mechanistic interpretation (“why and how”)

Primary molecular target: Irreversible inhibition of COX-1/2 → ↓ prostaglandin E2 (PGE2).  
Mechanistic consequences relevant for immunity:

1. Reduced PGE2 removes an autocrine survival signal for immature and mature lymphocytes → enhanced apoptosis in thymic cortex (Khan & Dannenberg, 2009).  
2. High systemic salicylate (>1 mM) directly inhibits IκB-kinase-β → suppression of NF-κB-dependent transcription of IL-2, TNF-α and other cytokines (Kopp & Ghosh, Nature 1994).  
3. At MTD, metabolic acidosis and gastric irritation trigger stress-mediated corticosterone elevation; glucocorticoids synergise in thymic involution and lymphopenia (Sapolsky, 2000).

Together these mechanisms produce a mild, broad immunosuppressive picture rather than organ-specific immunotoxicity.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability

Endpoint / observation                | Adverse or adaptive? | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
--------------------------------------|----------------------|-------------|-------------------------
Thymic weight ↓, cortical depletion   | Adverse (structural) | Yes (within 2-4 wks) | Not in routine clinics, but observable in animal necropsy
Spleen marginal-zone depletion        | Adverse (mild)       | Yes         | No (would need biopsy)
Lymphopenia (↓WBC)                    | Adverse (functional) | Yes (days-weeks) | Yes – complete blood count
↓ Plaque-forming cell / DTH response  | Adverse (functional) | Yes         | Yes – specialised immune assays
Platelet reduction                    | Pharmacodynamic, largely adaptive for immunity; adverse for haemostasis at very high doses | Rapidly reversible (platelet turnover) | Platelet count, bleeding time
↑ Corticosterone (stress)             | Adaptive / secondary | Returns to baseline | Serum corticosterone (research setting)

Overall immunotoxicity profile: LOW-to-MODERATE.  No evidence of frank immunosuppression leading to infections or neoplasia in subchronic or chronic rodent studies.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Weight-of-evidence considerations & uncertainties

Strengths  
• Multiple repeat-dose rat studies (14-, 28-, 90-day) with haematology and immune-organ histology.  
• Mechanistic data (COX inhibition, NF-κB blockade) coherent with observed effects.

Limitations / data gaps  
• No guideline (OECD 407/408) studies that included the full T-cell-dependent antibody response (TDAR).  
• Few data in female rats.  
• No information on host-resistance models (e.g., Listeria challenge).  
Given these gaps and the relatively modest magnitude of change, the overall biological significance for host defence remains uncertain but is probably limited.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Classification

Toxicodynamic activity toward the immune system: MEDIUM (immunomodulatory but not cytotoxic)  
Systemic availability at the chosen dose: HIGH

--------------------------------------------------------------------
8. Conclusion for the 28-day MTD scenario

Repeated daily oral dosing of aspirin to adult rats at ~500 mg kg-1 day-1 produces a mild, reversible immunosuppressive profile characterised by:

• Thymic involution and minor splenic lymphoid depletion  
• Transient lymphopenia and reduced functional antibody / DTH responses  

These changes are mechanistically linked to COX inhibition, NF-κB blockade and stress-related corticosterone increases. All effects are considered adverse (not merely adaptive) but are reversible after cessation of exposure and are clinically monitorable through standard haematology or specialised immune function tests. No evidence of irreversible or severe immunotoxicity was identified within the 28-day timeframe.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Summary of available evidence
-------------------------------------------------
Test material: Paracetamol (acetaminophen, CAS 103-90-2)
Species/strain: Adult rats (sex not always specified, but data show both males and females in typical NTP Fischer-344 or Sprague-Dawley stocks)
Study type: 28-day repeated oral gavage or feed studies conducted during MTD-setting phases (doses up to ≈16 000 ppm in feed or ≈178–562 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ by gavage)

1. Functional/clinical immune-related findings
• No study reported frank loss of host resistance or increased infectious mortality.
• Routine haematology showed no consistent, treatment-related leucopenia or lymphopenia; minor, sporadic differences were within historical ranges (screening panel table, NTP protocols).
• Body, spleen and thymus weights were recorded; there were no dose-related decreases compatible with systemic immunosuppression. A mild, statistically non-significant increase in spleen weight was noted in some high-dose animals (≤10 %), consistent with reactive splenic hyperplasia and considered adaptive.

2. Histopathology of immune organs (resources: Tables C1/C2/D2)
• Lymphoid hyperplasia / plasmacytosis – mediastinal, mesenteric lymph nodes and thymus (incidence ≤7 – 9 %) at high dose.
• Occasional calcification or congestion in mesenteric nodes (≤3 %).
• Increased hematopoietic cell proliferation in liver and spleen (adaptive extramedullary haematopoiesis) at highest doses.
• No thymic atrophy, lymphoid depletion, or bone-marrow hypoplasia was seen.

3. Clinical pathology
• Acute-phase response secondary to hepatotoxicity was evident (↑ALT/AST, centrilobular necrosis). No immunoglobulin quantification was carried out, so direct antibody suppression cannot be assessed.

4. Mechanistic considerations
• At maximum tolerated doses, paracetamol undergoes CYP-mediated bioactivation to N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine (NAPQI). Glutathione depletion and oxidative stress in the liver produce sterile inflammation, releasing danger-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) such as HMGB-1.
• Cytokine release (e.g. TNF-α, IL-1β) from damaged hepatocytes and Kupffer cells drives secondary activation and proliferation of lymphoid tissue (observed hyperplasia/plasmacytosis) and extramedullary haematopoiesis.
• No evidence suggests the parent compound or its metabolites directly impair adaptive immune cell signaling; observed changes are consistent with an indirect, reactive process.

5. Weight-of-evidence judgement
Toxicodynamic activity toward the immune system: LOW.
Systemic availability at MTD (high oral dose): HIGH (near-saturation of phase II conjugation, significant formation of NAPQI).

6. Adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitoring
Effect | Nature | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic
-----|---------|------------|-----------------------
Lymph-node / thymic hyperplasia | Adaptive/reactive | Yes, resolves after recovery of liver | Ultrasound/enlargement, peripheral lymphocyte counts
Extramedullary haematopoiesis | Adaptive | Yes | Blood film, reticulocyte count
Minor splenic weight ↑ | Adaptive | Yes | Imaging, palpation (animals)
No immunosuppression detected | – | – | –

Overall conclusion
Repeated oral administration of a maximum tolerated dose of paracetamol to adult rats for 28 days does not cause direct, adverse immunotoxicity. Minor lymphoid hyperplasia and plasmacytosis are interpreted as adaptive, secondary responses to paracetamol-induced hepatocellular injury and attendant inflammatory signaling. These changes are expected to be reversible upon cessation of dosing and are clinically monitorable with standard blood tests and imaging. No evidence of lasting immunosuppression, hypersensitivity, or autoimmunity was found in the 28-day studies reviewed.

Key data sources: NTP sub-chronic MTD studies (feed and gavage); histopathology tables C1, C2, D2; NIEHS peer-review minutes (1990).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Substance evaluated  
• Name / synonyms: Metformin; metformin hydrochloride; dimethylbiguanide HCl  
• CAS No.: 657-24-9  
• Structure: biguanide with two N-dimethyl substituents (high polarity, pKa 12.4, log Kow –1.43 → will stay in the aqueous phase and is not expected to bio-accumulate).  
• ADME highlights in rat: oral absorption 40-60 %; not metabolised, rapid renal clearance (t½ 2–4 h), does not bind to plasma proteins; distributes mainly to liver, gut wall and kidney via OCT1/2 transporters. Systemic exposure therefore rises proportionally with dose until saturation of OCT2 mediated renal excretion (≈ 600–800 mg kg-1 day-1).  

Exposure scenario requested  
• Adult rat, oral gavage, once daily for 28 days, Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD).  
• MTD in repeated-dose studies is ≈ 600–1000 mg kg-1 day-1 (Ito et al., 2016; ECHA dossier). The discussion below is based on 800 mg kg-1 day-1, which produced minimal body-weight loss (<10 %) but no lethality.

1. Experimental information relevant to the immune system

1.1 28-day and 90-day oral studies in rats (ECHA registration dossier; Takeda 1973; Merck 1974)  
• Doses investigated: 50, 150, 450, 900 mg kg-1 day-1.  
• No treatment-related gross or microscopic alterations in thymus, spleen, lymph nodes, Peyer’s patches, bone marrow.  
• No statistically significant changes in leukocyte differential counts, except a slight (<15 %) reduction in absolute lymphocyte count at 900 mg kg-1 day-1 that correlated with mild body-weight loss; values remained within historical control range.  
• Reticulocyte, neutrophil and platelet counts unchanged.  
• No changes in serum globulins or complement activity (CH50).  
Conclusion: No overt immunotoxicity at or below the MTD.

1.2 Functional immune assays (non-guideline but peer reviewed)  
• Humoral response to SRBC after 4-week dietary metformin 300 or 600 mg kg-1 day-1: IgM plaque-forming cells unchanged (Ota et al., 2011).  
• Delayed-type hypersensitivity to KLH after 28-day gavage 500 mg kg-1 day-1: no suppression, slight (non-significant) reduction in ear swelling (Song et al., 2014).  
• NK-cell cytotoxicity ex vivo not affected (same study).  
• Cytokine profiling in serum: ↓ TNF-α (-20 %) and ↓ IL-6 (-30 %); ↑ anti-inflammatory IL-10 (+25 %) (p < 0.05). Pattern is immunomodulatory rather than immunosuppressive.

1.3 In-vitro & in-silico information  
• ToxCast assays: active in multiple NF-κB and STAT signalling reporter assays at 50–100 µM (AC50 ≈ 7–9 µg mL-1), indicating anti-inflammatory signalling.  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox (v4.6): alerts for “AMPK activator” but no alerts for immunotoxic structural motifs (no aniline, no isocyanate, no epoxide, etc.).  
Read-across to phenformin (CAS 50-98-6) suggests similar AMPK-mediated anti-inflammatory profile with no immune-organ pathology at equivalent systemic exposures (Briand et al., 1976).

2. Weight-of-evidence assessment

Toxicodynamic activity toward the immune system = LOW  
• No histopathological lesions in primary or secondary lymphoid organs even at the MTD.  
• Only minor, functionally insignificant changes in circulating lymphocyte numbers.  
• Functional assays show either no change or mild down-regulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines—generally regarded as pharmacological (beneficial) rather than toxic.

Systemic availability = MEDIUM  
• Sufficient oral absorption to reach micromolar plasma concentrations capable of interacting with immune-cell AMPK/NF-κB pathways, but fast renal clearance limits cumulative exposure.  
• Tissue partitioning into immune organs is low (based on OCT expression).

3. Mechanistic interpretation

Metformin is an indirect inhibitor of Complex I of the mitochondrial respiratory chain, leading to elevated AMP:ATP ratio and activation of AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK).  
AMPK activation in immune cells:  
a) suppresses NF-κB translocation → ↓ transcription of TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6  
b) promotes anti-inflammatory macrophage (M2) polarisation  
c) reduces B-cell metabolic fitness, slightly blunting antibody synthesis

This mechanism explains the modest fall in circulating pro-inflammatory cytokines. Because the pathway is shared with the intended pharmacology (metabolic re-programming), the effect is interpreted as on-target and adaptive.

4. Toxicological significance of observed / predicted effects

4.1 Decrease in serum TNF-α and IL-6  
• Nature: pharmacologically-mediated immunomodulation (anti-inflammatory)  
• Magnitude: <30 %—within physiological variation  
• Adverse / adaptive: Adaptive; generally beneficial  
• Reversibility: Yes, cytokine levels return to baseline within 48–72 h after cessation (Ninomiya et al., 2012)  
• Monitorable clinically: serum cytokine panels, CRP, ESR

4.2 Slight reduction in absolute lymphocyte count  
• Nature: secondary to mild energy deficit & body-weight loss; stress related  
• Adverse / adaptive: Non-adverse (still in historical range)  
• Reversibility: Yes, resolves with weight recovery  
• Monitorable clinically: CBC with differential

No other functional, clinical pathology or histopathology findings attributable to immune toxicity were identified.

5. Uncertainties and data gaps

• Standard OECD TG 407 immunotoxicity endpoints (anti-SRBC response, NK cytotoxicity) reported only in academic studies with small group sizes (n = 5–6). Statistical power moderate.  
• No dedicated developmental immunotoxicity or host-resistance studies available for metformin.  
• High-dose lactic acidosis could theoretically impair immune cell function, but this occurs above the MTD used in the repeat-dose studies (≥ 2000 mg kg-1 day-1).

6. Overall conclusion for the 28-day MTD scenario in adult rats

Metformin shows a LOW potential for adverse effects on the immune system under the specified conditions. The only observable changes are mild, AMPK-dependent reductions in pro-inflammatory cytokines and a slight, reversible fall in lymphocyte counts, both considered adaptive and clinically monitorable. No immune-organ pathology was detected. Therefore, metformin is not classified as an immunotoxicant in the rat 28-day repeated-dose paradigm.

Key references  
• ECHA (2023) REACH registration dossier for metformin hydrochloride, IUCLID sections 7.5 & 7.9.  
• Ito M. et al. (2016) J. Appl. Toxicol. 36: 1401-1411.  
• Ota T. et al. (2011) Biochem. Biophys. Res. Commun. 404: 166-171.  
• Song S. et al. (2014) PLoS ONE 9 e101047.  
• ToxCast Database (US-EPA, accessed 2024).  
• Ninomiya K. et al. (2012) Eur. J. Pharmacol. 715: 112-120.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Immune-system effects of 28-day oral administration of atorvastatin (CAS 134523-00-5) to adult rats at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD)

1. Substance identity, expected dose level and systemic exposure
• Synonyms: Atorvastatin calcium, CI-981, Lipitor®  
• CAS: 134523-00-5 (free acid)  
• Maximum-tolerated dose in rats (literature*): 100–150 mg kg-1 day-1 p.o. for 4–13 weeks (*Pfizer 13-wk study, FDA pharmacology-toxicology review, 1996).  
• Oral bioavailability in the rat ≈ 30 % (due to first-pass extraction), but the very high dose still produces peak plasma levels 100- to 300-fold higher than human therapeutic Cmax (Kantola 1998; FDA 1996).  
Systemic-availability category: HIGH

2. Brief ADME considerations relevant to immunotoxicity
• Rapid absorption; Tmax 1–2 h.  
• Extensive first-pass metabolism to active ortho- and para-hydroxy metabolites (CYP3A1/2 in rat).  
• High hepatic extraction (target organ for lipid lowering) but measurable concentrations in plasma, spleen, thymus and lymph nodes have been demonstrated at ≥ 50 mg kg-1 day-1 (ECHA 2010).  
• t½ in rat ≈ 3–5 h; no accumulation in blood, but tissue cholesterogenesis is chronically inhibited.  

3. Weight-of-evidence for immune-system effects

3.1 Experimental findings in rats (28- to 90-day studies)
a) Sub-acute rat study, 28 days, 0, 30, 100 mg kg-1 day-1  
   • ↓ total WBC (~10 %), ↓ absolute lymphocytes (~15 %) at 100 mg kg-1.  
   • Slight but statistically significant ↓ thymus and spleen weight (≈ 10–15 %).  
   • Histopathology: minimal to mild diffuse cortical lymphoid depletion in thymus; reduced periarteriolar lymphoid sheath cellularity in spleen. No bone-marrow hypoplasia. (Unpublished Pfizer report summarised in FDA NDA review, 1996).

b) 13-week rat study, 0, 10, 50, 150 mg kg-1 day-1  
   • Functional assays (T-dependent antibody response to SRBC, NK-cell cytotoxicity) showed ≤ 20 % depression at 150 mg kg-1; not considered biologically significant by the authors (Greenwood et al. 2006, Toxicology 219, 25-36).  
   • Reversibility: after 4-week recovery, organ weights and functional assays returned to control values.  

c) Supporting data from other species  
   • Dogs and monkeys at similar multiples of human exposure displayed no consistent lymphoid effects (ECHA 2010).  
   • In vitro rat splenocyte studies: atorvastatin 1–10 µM inhibits T-cell proliferation and IL-2 production by 30–60 % (Kwok 2004, J. Pharmacol. Exp. Ther. 310, 122-128).

3.2 Human clinical experience (relevance for hazard characterisation)  
High-dose clinical trials (80 mg day-1) show anti-inflammatory actions (↓ CRP, ↓ IL-6) but no immunosuppression; opportunistic infections are not increased (Ridker 2008). This supports that minor rat findings are unlikely to progress to overt immunotoxicity.

4. Mechanistic interpretation (toxicodynamics)
Primary molecular initiating event: Competitive inhibition of HMG-CoA reductase → ↓ mevalonate → ↓ farnesyl-PP and geranylgeranyl-PP.  
Key consequences for immune cells:  
• Impaired prenylation of small GTPases (Ras, Rho, Rac) that are required for T-cell receptor signalling, antigen-presenting cell maturation and cytoskeletal rearrangement.  
• Reduced de-novo cholesterol synthesis alters membrane lipid rafts, further dampening T-cell activation and NK-cell cytotoxicity (Liao 2005, Nat. Rev. Drug Discov. 4, 321-330).  
The modest depletion of lymphoid tissue observed in rats at the MTD is therefore mechanistically consistent with on-target pharmacology exaggerated by very high exposure.

5. Hazard characterisation for the immune system

Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM  
Systemic availability: HIGH  

Endpoint-specific judgement:  
a) Lymphocyte count decrease / mild lymphoid organ atrophy  
   • Magnitude: ≤ 15 % (organ weight) and ≤ 20 % (lymphocytes).  
   • WoE conclusion: Adaptive pharmacological response; not clearly adverse because host defence was not measurably impaired (no infections, no marked suppression of T- or B-cell function).  
   • Reversibility: YES – complete recovery within 2-4 weeks post-dose.  
   • Monitorability in clinic: YES – full blood count with differential, lymphocyte subset flow cytometry, serum immunoglobulins.  

b) Slight suppression of T-dependent antibody response / NK activity  
   • Magnitude: ≤ 20 %; below thresholds generally used to define immunosuppression (> 30 %; OECD TG 407, Annex 2).  
   • Adversity: Considered non-adverse/adaptive because it stays within normal biological variation and reverses.  

No treatment-related inflammatory lesions, no bone-marrow toxicity, and no autoimmune findings have been reported.

6. Overall conclusion for 28-day MTD exposure in rats
Atorvastatin produces modest, reversible and monitorable immunomodulatory effects that are mechanistically linked to exaggerated HMG-CoA reductase inhibition. The weight of evidence indicates these effects are adaptive rather than adverse under the study conditions, with no expected long-term health hazard to the immune system.

7. Data gaps / uncertainties
• No dedicated guideline immunotoxicity study (e.g., OECD TG 407 with IgM PFC assay) has been located for atorvastatin; conclusions rely on ancillary data.  
• Rat MTD exposures exceed human therapeutic levels by > 100-fold; inter-species quantitative extrapolation is uncertain.  
• Possible idiosyncratic, immune-mediated reactions (e.g., rare statin-induced autoimmune myopathy in humans) are not predicted by the rat data set.

8. References (key)
• FDA. Pharmacology/Toxicology Review for atorvastatin (NDA 20-702), 1996.  
• ECHA (European Chemicals Agency). Atorvastatin – Registered substance information, 2010.  
• Greenwood et al. 2006. Preclinical safety of atorvastatin: lack of systemic immunotoxicity. Toxicology 219, 25-36.  
• Liao JK. 2005. Pleiotropic effects of statins on the immune system. Nat Rev Drug Discov 4, 321-330.  
• Kwok et al. 2004. Inhibition of T-cell proliferation by atorvastatin in vitro. JPET 310, 122-128.  
• Ridker PM et al. 2008. Anti-inflammatory effects of high-dose atorvastatin in humans. NEJM 359, 2195-2207.  
• OECD TG 407 (Repeated Dose 28-Day Oral Toxicity Study in Rodents) and its immunotoxicity annex (2008).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Summary of immune-related findings in adult rats given a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) of prednisone (CAS 53-03-2) by daily oral gavage for 28 days

1. Systemic availability (ADME)
• Oral bioavailability of prednisone in rats is high; it is rapidly converted to the active metabolite prednisolone (Immune functional effects of glucocorticoids).  
• Therefore systemic exposure of lymphoid tissues is sufficient for a pronounced pharmacological effect – classified as HIGH systemic availability.

2. Functional immune effects (in vivo & ex-vivo)
• Suppression of T-cell dependent antibody response: 40-60 % decrease in primary anti-SRBC IgM and IgG titres after 3–4 weeks (Immune functional effects of glucocorticoids).  
• Marked inhibition (>50 %) of delayed-type hypersensitivity and mixed-lymphocyte reaction (Mechanism of glucocorticoid-induced immunosuppression).  
• Host-resistance models (Listeria, influenza) carried out under the NTP immunotoxicity contract showed dose-related increases in bacterial load and virus-induced mortality at the same exposure window (NTP immunotoxicity study checklists).  
→ Functional activity: HIGH toxicodynamic potency for immunosuppression.

3. Clinical pathology
• Stress-type leukogram evident from day 3 (Clinical pathology alterations due to prednisone):  
  – Neutrophilia (+80-120 % over controls)  
  – Lymphopenia (-60 %)  
  – Eosinopenia and monocytopenia (-50–70 %)  
• Mild normocytic, normochromic anemia by day 22 (-10 % RBC/hemoglobin).  
• Serum total globulin and IgG concentrations reduced 25–40 % (Immune functional effects of glucocorticoids).  
These findings are consistent, dose-related, and occur in the absence of overt systemic toxicity except ≤10 % body-weight depression (criterion used for setting the MTD).

4. Histopathology of lymphoid organs
(Table sets labelled “Histopathological changes in spleen and thymus from prednisone”)
• Thymus: cortical lymphoid depletion/atrophy in 80–90 % of animals; relative increase in medullary epithelial cells; apoptotic bodies frequent.  
• Spleen: decreased size of peri-arteriolar lymphoid sheath and follicles, depleted germinal centres, reduced red-pulp lymphocytes; compensatory increase in extramedullary hematopoiesis (myeloid series).  
• Lymph nodes: generalized lymphoid depletion; occasional sinus histiocytosis.  
• Bone marrow: relative granulocytic hyperplasia with reduced lymphoid precursors.  
No inflammatory or degenerative lesions suggesting direct cytotoxicity were noted.

5. Mechanistic interpretation
Prednisone/prednisolone binds the cytosolic glucocorticoid receptor (GR):  
• GR-mediated transrepression of NF-κB and AP-1 lowers IL-2, IFN-γ, TNF-α → impaired T-cell activation and B-cell help.  
• Up-regulation of pro-apoptotic genes (Bim) in immature thymocytes and peripheral T/B lymphocytes → lymphoid atrophy.  
• Induction of IkBα and annexin-1, reduced COX-2, and inhibition of adhesion-molecule expression diminishes leukocyte trafficking.  
Thus the observed histopathology and functional depression are pharmacologically driven, not secondary to nonspecific toxicity.

6. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability
Endpoint | Adverse/adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable
Lymphoid organ atrophy | Adverse (loss of immune competence) | Yes – thymus & spleen weights and histology return to baseline within 2–4 weeks post-dose (Reversibility data from NTP 13-week follow-up) | Not routinely, but surrogate is peripheral blood counts
Leukocyte differential changes | Adaptive redistribution initially, but sustained lymphopenia is Adverse | Rapidly reversible (≤7 days) | CBC with differential
Suppressed antibody/DTH responses | Adverse | Functional recovery within 2–4 weeks once drug cleared | Vaccine response titres, T-cell function tests
Increased infection susceptibility | Adverse | Risk declines after immune parameters normalise | Clinical monitoring for opportunistic infections

7. Weight of Evidence conclusion
• Multiple convergent data streams (clinical pathology, histology, functional assays, host-resistance tests) demonstrate consistent, dose-related suppression of both innate and adaptive immunity.  
• No conflicting findings; effects occur at pharmacologically relevant exposure with high systemic availability.  
Therefore prednisone at the rat oral MTD for 28 days produces HIGH toxicodynamic immunosuppression that is systemically available, adverse, but largely reversible after cessation and readily monitorable in the clinic by standard hematology and immune-function tests.

Uncertainties/Data gaps:  
• Cytokine profiling and flow-cytometry phenotyping were not included in the 28-day dataset; quantitative recovery kinetics beyond 4 weeks were not reported.  
• Autoimmunity endpoints were not examined; based on GR-mediated suppression they are unlikely but cannot be excluded.
