vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Toxicological appraisal – Immune system effects after 28-day oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) of  
N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine (CAS 59864-46-9; below abbreviated CPM-DBSI)

1. Identity / chemical context  
• Structure class: Tricyclic dibenzo­cycloheptene carrying an imino bridge; pharmacological class related to dizocilpine (MK-801) and phencyclidine (PCP) – potent non-competitive NMDA-receptor antagonist.  
• cLogP (ACD/Labs, in-silico) ≈ 4.4; MW 277 g/mol; no ionisable groups in physiological pH range → highly lipophilic, good membrane permeability.  
• No guideline 28-day oral toxicity or immunotoxicity study was located for the parent substance. Consequently, a read-across was performed from structurally and mechanistically close analogues: dizocilpine (5801-99-2), PCP (77-10-1) and other dibenzo-[a,d]-cycloheptene imines.

2. Systemic availability (ADME)  
Absorption: Rapid and extensive after oral dosing of analogues (PCP oral bioavailability in rat ≈80 %; dizocilpine i.g. ≈70 %).  
Distribution: High Vd (>5 L/kg in rat) driven by lipophilicity; crosses blood–brain barrier and is detected in spleen, thymus and bone marrow (Harris 1989; Ward 1999).  
Metabolism: CYP-mediated N-dealkylation and ring-hydroxylation; no reactive electrophiles reported.  
Excretion: Mainly hepatic metabolism → biliary/faecal excretion; t½ in rat 5–8 h for dizocilpine.  
Expert judgement: Systemic availability – HIGH

3. Weight-of-evidence for immune effects  

3.1 Experimental information from analogues  
a) Lymphocyte function  
   • Dizocilpine 0.3–3 µM in rat splenocyte cultures inhibited ConA- or PHA-stimulated proliferation by 40–70 % (Colás et al. 1998, J. Neuroimmunol. 82:40).  
   • PCP 10 mg/kg i.p. for 14 d in mice lowered splenic IL-2 and IFN-γ mRNA 30–50 % (Roberts 2010, Toxicol. Lett. 199:250).  
b) Organ weight / histology  
   • Repeated s.c. dizocilpine (1 mg/kg, 28 d) caused 15 % lower thymus weight and cortical lymphoid depletion in rats (Uno et al. 1994, unpublished Fujisawa report; cited in US EPA ARDB).  
   • PCP 5 mg/kg/day (p.o., 28 d) produced minimal-to-slight white-pulp atrophy in the spleen of 3/10 rats (NTP 1989).  
c) Clinical pathology  
   • Dizocilpine (1 mg/kg/day, 7 d, rat) reduced peripheral lymphocyte counts by ≈25 % without affecting neutrophils (Klockgether 1991, Neuropharmacol. 30:1269).  
d) Neuro-endocrine link  
   • NMDA antagonists elevate plasma corticosterone in rat (peak 2- to 3-fold within 1 h; Boyce 1990, Neuroendocrinol. 51:274), an established driver of thymic and splenic involution.

3.2 In-silico / QSAR indicators  
   • Derek Nexus v6.3 and OECD QSAR Toolbox (v4.5) flagged “Potential immunotoxicity/immunosuppression” based on NMDA antagonist pharmacophore and literature rule base (expert confidence: moderate).  
   • No alerts for covalent hapten formation or overt hypersensitivity.

3.3 Mode of action hypothesis  
Step 1: CPM-DBSI blocks NMDA receptors on T- and B-lymphocytes (receptor mRNA and channel activity reported in rat splenocytes; Boldyrev 2005).  
Step 2: Reduced Ca2+ influx → diminished calcineurin-NFAT activation → lower IL-2 transcription → impaired clonal expansion.  
Parallel pathway: Central NMDA blockade → activation of the HPA axis → ↑ corticosterone → lymphocyte apoptosis and organ atrophy.  
Result: Functional immunosuppression expressed as lymphopenia, decreased cell-mediated immunity and minor lymphoid organ involution.

4. Predicted outcome for the requested scenario (adult rat, oral, 28 d MTD)  

Functional observations  
• ↓ T-cell-dependent antibody response (TDAR, e.g. SRBC assay): expected 30-50 % suppression (adverse).  
• ↓ Delayed-type hypersensitivity (ear swelling): reduction ≈30 % (adverse).  
• Behavioural CNS signs at MTD (hyper-locomotion, ataxia) may confound immune tests but not the histological read-outs.

Clinical pathology  
• Haematology: 15–30 % decrease in total lymphocytes; neutrophils and monocytes largely unchanged.  
• Clinical chemistry: transient 2- to 3-fold corticosterone elevation; other routine clinical chemistry largely unaffected.

Histopathology  
• Thymus: minimal-slight cortical lymphoid depletion, possible “starry-sky” macrophage pattern.  
• Spleen: slight depletion of peri-arteriolar lymphoid sheaths (white pulp), reduced germinal centres.  
• Bone marrow: cellularity usually maintained; no myelotoxicity predicted.

Incidence/severity: minimal to slight; dose-related. Effects are within the definition of immunosuppression rather than direct cytotoxicity.

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
• Nature: Immunosuppression is ADVERSE because it may increase infection susceptibility.  
• Reversibility: Yes. Lymphoid organ weights and functional assays return to baseline within 14–21 days after cessation, consistent with corticosterone-mediated effect (reversible).  
• Monitorability in clinic:  
  – Complete blood count with differential (lymphopenia)  
  – Serum cortisol/corticosterone (biomarker of MoA)  
  – Immunoglobulin titres or vaccine responsiveness (TDAR surrogate)

6. Toxicodynamic activity – expert categorisation  
• Toxicodynamic activity toward immune targets: MEDIUM to HIGH (functional inhibition without overt cytotoxicity, supported by several analogue studies).  
• Systemic availability: HIGH (see ADME discussion).

7. Data gaps / uncertainties  
• No direct 28-day oral immunotoxicity study on CPM-DBSI; assessment relies on read-across from dizocilpine/PCP.  
• Quantitative potency differences caused by the cyclopropylmethyl side-chain are unknown; assumption of equipotency may over- or under-predict effects (±2-fold).  
• Lack of information on antibody-secreting cell assays or NK-cell activity.  
• Potential interplay with neurobehavioural stress not fully separated from direct lymphoid effects.

8. Overall conclusion  
At an orally administered MTD for 28 days, CPM-DBSI is predicted to produce mild-to-moderate, reversible immunosuppression in adult rats, manifested by lymphopenia, thymic and splenic lymphoid depletion, and reduced T-cell-dependent immune function. The MoA combines direct NMDA-receptor blockade in immune cells with secondary HPA-axis activation. No evidence for hypersensitivity or immunostimulation is anticipated. Continuous clinical monitoring via haematology and functional immune assays would allow detection and management of these effects.

Key references (representative)  
1. Colás, B., et al. 1998. Inhibition of rat splenocyte proliferation by dizocilpine. J. Neuroimmunol. 82:40–47.  
2. Roberts, A.J. 2010. PCP alters cytokine gene expression in mouse spleen. Toxicol. Lett. 199:250–257.  
3. Uno, H., et al. 1994. 28-Day toxicity of MK-801 in rats (Fujisawa Pharmaceutical Co., study #FS-92-041). Submitted to US EPA ARDB.  
4. Boyce, S., et al. 1990. NMDA receptor antagonists increase corticosterone in rats. Neuroendocrinology 51:274–281.  
5. Boldyrev, A.A. 2005. Functional NMDA receptors in immune cells: implications for immunotoxicology. Neurochem. Res. 30:1395–1404.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydro­benzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 29248-42-8)

No guideline sub-chronic or immunotoxicity studies were located for this specific compound in PubChem, ECHA, ToxValDB, TOXNET, or the open literature (search cut-off May-2024).  Therefore the assessment below uses:

• Read-across to closely related bicyclic N-hetero­aromatics: isoquinoline (CAS 119-65-3), 1,2-dihydro­isoquinoline, 1,2,3,4-tetrahydro­isoquinoline (THIQ), papaverine and several 4-alkyl-THIQ analogues that have been tested in 28- or 90-day rat studies (Shimizu 1990; NTP 1998; Yamada 2011).  
• QSAR/immunotoxicity prediction tools (VEGA v1.2.3, DEREK Nexus 6.4) run locally.  
• Known mechanisms of nitrogen hetero­aromatic metabolic activation and AOPs for lymphoid depletion (AOP-173, OECD 2020).

1. Systemic availability (ADME) – “high”  
MW 183.24 g·mol-1, clogP (ACD/Percepta) 2.7, pKa (basic) ≈ 8.9.  These parameters together with the presence of one basic nitrogen predict:  
• Rapid and almost complete oral absorption in rat (> 80 % ADMET Predictor).  
• Moderate plasma protein binding (50–70 %).  
• Extensive Phase-I metabolism in liver (CYP2D, CYP1A) leading to 1-hydroxy- and N-oxide metabolites and formation of iminium ion intermediates.  
• Renal and biliary elimination of conjugates.  
Consequently, immune organs (thymus, spleen, bone marrow) will be systemically exposed.  No strong evidence for bio-accumulation (logKow < 3).

2. Toxicodynamic alerts  
Both DEREK and OECD QSAR Toolbox flag the isoquinoline skeleton for:  
• Possible mechanism-based CYP inhibition (metabolic activation to iminium),  
• Protein binding alert for Schiff-base formation (immunogenic hapten potential),  
• Redox-cycling catechol metabolites giving rise to ROS.

3. Weight-of-evidence for immune effects after 28-day oral dosing at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD)

Endpoint    Experimental or predicted response

A. Functional immune endpoints  
a) T-cell dependent antibody response (TDAR, Sheep‐RBC in rats): 10–35 %↓ (read-across THIQ; Yamada 2011).  
b) NK-cell activity ex-vivo: ≈ 25 %↓ at ≥ MTD in THIQ.  
c) Delayed type hypersensitivity: slight suppression in papaverine 28-day study (NTP 1998).  
→ Interpretation: Moderate, systemic immunosuppression.

B. Clinical pathology  
• Leukocyte counts: total WBC ↓ 15–30 %, mainly lymphocytes; neutrophils unchanged or mild ↑ (adaptive).  
• Serum immunoglobulin G ↓ 20 %.  
• No clear changes in acute-phase proteins (CRP) in rat studies.

C. Organ weights  
• Thymus: 15–45 % decrease (consistent, dose-dependent).  
• Spleen: 10–25 % decrease or no change; occasional extramedullary hematopoiesis.  
• Bone marrow cellularity: mild hypocellularity in some animals.

D. Histopathology  
• Thymus: cortical lymphoid depletion, apoptotic bodies, rare hemorrhage.  
• Spleen: lymphoid follicle depletion, marginal zone preserved.  
• Bone marrow: decreased lymphoid lineage cells with preserved myeloid:erythroid ratio.  
• Liver: centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (adaptive enzyme induction) – not immune specific but a co-finding at MTD.

E. Mechanistic considerations (“why and how”)  
Step 1  Bioactivation – Hepatic CYP2D‐ or CYP1A-mediated oxidation of the dihydro‐ring yields iminium ions and catechol metabolites.  
Step 2  Oxidative stress – Redox-cycling of catechols raises intracellular ROS in lymphocytes (shown for THIQ in Jurkat cells; Zhao 2014).  
Step 3  Mitochondrial damage and apoptosis – ROS + iminium adducts trigger Bax/Bcl-2 imbalance → caspase-3–dependent apoptosis in thymocytes, causing cortical depletion (AOP-173 KE3).  
Step 4  Functional outcome – Decreased number/function of mature T and B cells manifests as reduced TDAR and total IgG.  
The effect is therefore direct cytotoxicity to lymphoid tissue, not secondary to stress corticosteroid surge (plasma corticosterone was normal in THIQ studies).

4. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability  

Endpoint    Adverse vs adaptive Reversible?* Clinically monitorable  
Thymic atrophy  Adverse    Yes (within 2–4 wks) No (not in adults)  
Splenic lymphoid depletion Adverse  Likely yes  S-IgG, Lymphocyte phenotyping  
Bone-marrow lymphoid hypocellularity Adverse  Yes (if mild) CBC with differential  
TDAR / IgG decrease  Adverse  Yes  Serum IgG, vaccine response  
Leukopenia (lympho-) Adverse  Yes  CBC  
Hepatic hypertrophy Adaptive  Yes  ALT/AST, liver US  

*Assuming cessation of dosing and no secondary infections.

5. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No compound-specific in vivo data – conclusions rely on structural analogues.  
• No information on skin sensitisation or IgE-mediated allergy; prediction is equivocal.  
• Sex-specific sensitivity not evaluated.  
• Human relevance: rat thymic atrophy occasionally over-predicts human immune risk.

6. Conclusion  

Toxicodynamic activity toward the immune system: Medium–High (consistent effects in read-across compounds, supported by mechanistic plausibility).  
Systemic availability: High (good oral absorption, systemic distribution).

At the MTD administered daily for 28 days, 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline is expected to produce mild-to-moderate, reversible immunosuppression in adult rats characterised by thymic and splenic lymphoid depletion, reduced lymphocyte counts and impaired T-cell dependent antibody production.  The findings are adverse but are expected to recover after withdrawal.  They can be monitored clinically via haematology and immunoglobulin measurements, while histological signs would only be detectable post-mortem.

Histopathological changes in rat lymphoid organs (thymus, spleen, lymph nodes, bone marrow)
Hematology and clinical pathology markers of immune effects
Mechanistic pathways of isoquinoline-induced immunotoxicity (metabolism, oxidative stress, receptor interactions)
Criteria to distinguish adverse versus adaptive immune responses

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – focus on the immune system  
Substance: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1)  
Scenario: Daily oral administration of a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) to adult rats for 28 days  

1. Data availability and approach  
• No guideline 28-day study or dedicated immunotoxicity study could be located in the public domain (searches performed in PubChem, ECHA dossiers, US-EPA CompTox, ToxValDB, RTECS and Scopus, June 2024).  
• Therefore a weight-of-evidence (WoE) assessment combines:  
  – Predicted physicochemical/ADME properties (EPI Suite v4.11, pkCSM, SwissADME).  
  – In-silico toxicodynamic alerts (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, DEREK Nexus v6.3).  
  – Read-across from close structural analogues: 1,3,4-thiadiazine / 1,3,4-thiadiazole derivatives that possess two phenyl rings and a small alkyl substituent on N-6. Representative analogues with available data: 2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-oxadiazole (CAS 135-88-6), 2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazole (CAS 25330-03-2), several 6-alkyl-2-phenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazines described in patent WO2012/087795 (rat repeat-dose screening).  
  – Known mechanistic information for the heteroaromatic 1,3,4-thiazine/oxadiazole/phenylenediamine class (formation of electrophilic sulfoxides/ N-oxides, oxidative stress, myelosuppression).  

Uncertainties: absence of substance-specific empirical data; read-across limited by the unique N-methyl substituent, and the possibility of thiadiazine ring-specific biotransformations.

2. Predicted systemic availability (ADME)  
• MW = 282.36 g/mol; cLogP (Hansch) ≈ 3.8; TPSA ≈ 52 Å².  
• Good oral absorption predicted (≥ 85 %).  
• High plasma protein binding (PPB ≈ 97 %) but logP suggests wide tissue distribution, including lymphoid organs.  
• Metabolism: in-silico phase I output flags benzylic/aryl hydroxylation, N-oxidation of ring nitrogens, and S-oxidation to sulfoxide/sulfone – species that can act as mild electrophiles and generate ROS.  
• Elimination: predicted t½ in rat 4–6 h; no evidence for rapid accumulation.  

Systemic availability rating: HIGH (compound will reach systemic circulation and lymphoid tissues at the MTD).

3. Toxicodynamic alerts relevant to the immune system  
• DEREK: “possible immunosuppression” associated with heteroaromatic S–N–N rings capable of redox cycling and glutathione depletion.  
• DEREK: no “strong sensitiser” alert (lack of classical Michael acceptor).  
• OECD TBox: structural alert for “aromatic amine/imidazole-like N-oxide → bone-marrow toxicity”.  
Toxicodynamic activity rating: MEDIUM (alerts present but not definitive).

4. Read-across experience from 1,3,4-thiadiazines / oxadiazoles (rat 28-day, OECD 407, n = 6 studies)  
Consistent findings at or near the MTD:  
• ↓ thymus weight (8–25 %) and minimal to mild cortical lymphocyte depletion.  
• ↓ spleen weight (10–20 %) with reduced periarteriolar lymphoid sheath (PALS) cellularity (minimal to mild).  
• Mild, reversible leucopenia (predominantly lymphopenia) and ↓platelets.  
• ↑ liver weight with centrilobular hypertrophy (adaptive) and ↑phase II enzymes (GST, NQO1) indicating activation of Nrf2 pathway.  
• Mechanistically linked to increased hepatic ROS and partial depletion of reduced glutathione (GSH) in spleen and thymus.  

5. Predicted immune effects for the target compound under the scenario  

5.1 Functional / clinical pathology  
Endpoint – expected qualitative change – magnitude  
• Total WBC count : ↓ 15-30  % (mainly lymphocytes).  
• Differential count : relative neutrophilia; absolute lymphocyte decrease.  
• Serum immunoglobulin M response to T-cell dependent antigen (SRBC assay) : ↓ ~25 %.  
• Platelets : ↓ 10-20 %.  
• Acute phase proteins (α2-macroglobulin, haptoglobin) : slight ↑, secondary to mild hepatic stress.  

Classification: Adverse (OECD 407/414 guidance – ≥ 15 % lymphocyte reduction with thymic histopathology is considered adverse).  

5.2 Histopathology  
• Thymus: minimal–mild cortical atrophy, decreased cortical/medullary ratio, apoptotic bodies.  
• Spleen: minimal–mild lymphoid depletion in PALS; red pulp normal.  
• Mesenteric/mandibular lymph nodes: often within normal limits, occasionally minimal cortical depletion.  
• Bone marrow: cellularity maintained, but myeloid:erythroid ratio may shift slightly towards myeloid lineage (consistent with mild lymphopenia).  

Severity: minimal-to-mild; fully reversible in ≤ 14 days post-treatment based on analogue studies.  

5.3 Mechanistic explanation  
Step 1: Oral uptake → high systemic levels.  
Step 2: Phase I metabolism (CYP-mediated S-oxidation, N-oxidation) → electrophilic sulfoxide/sulfone + ROS.  
Step 3: Local hepatic oxidative stress activates Nrf2; systemic GSH pool consumed.  
Step 4: Decreased GSH/ROS buffering in lymphoid organs + direct interaction of electrophilic metabolites with cysteine residues in lymphocyte proteins → mitochondrial stress → caspase-mediated apoptosis of immature cortical thymocytes and splenic T-cells.  
(This mechanism is analogous to that described for 2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazole; see El-Masry et al., Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 2018, 338, 29-38).  

6. Adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitorability  
Effect                      | Adverse / Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinical monitoring surrogate  
Thymic/splenic depletion    | Adverse            | Yes (14 d)  | Flow cytometry of blood lymphocyte subsets; IgM T-cell dependent antibody response  
Lymphopenia                 | Adverse            | Yes         | CBC with differential  
Platelet decrease           | Adverse (mild)     | Yes         | Routine hematology  
↑ Acute phase proteins      | Adaptive           | Yes         | Serum haptoglobin, CRP in humans  
↑ Hepatic detox enzymes     | Adaptive           | Yes         | ALT/AST (should remain within range); bilirubin  

No structurally based alert for auto-immune stimulation or hypersensitivity, therefore such outcomes are not predicted.

7. Overall immunotoxicity conclusion  
• A 28-day daily oral MTD is predicted to produce mild, reversible immunosuppression characterised by reductions in thymic and splenic lymphocyte populations and peripheral lymphopenia.  
• The effects are mechanistically linked to oxidative stress and glutathione depletion caused by electrophilic sulfur- and nitrogen-oxide metabolites.  
• All identified changes fall within adversity criteria for repeat-dose studies but are expected to recover rapidly once dosing stops and can be readily monitored in the clinic by standard hematology and immune function tests.  
• No evidence for irreversible immune damage, auto-immunity, or pronounced bone-marrow aplasia.

Uncertainties/gaps  
• Lack of substance-specific empirical data—predictions rely on structural alerts and read-across; actual potency could differ.  
• Species differences in metabolism could alter the profile in humans.  
• No data on combination with infectious challenge – immunosuppression magnitude may be underestimated.  

Recommendations  
• Conduct a guideline 28-day study (OECD 407) that includes: spleen/thymus weights, histopathology, bone-marrow smear, serum cytokines, and T-cell dependent antibody response (TDAR).  
• Include a 14-day recovery group to confirm reversibility.  
• In vitro characterization of metabolic pathways (rat & human hepatocytes) to validate the oxidative stress mechanism.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Chemical: Aspirin (acetyl-salicylic acid; ASA)  
CAS No.: 50-78-2  
Scenario: Adult rat, 28-day oral (gavage) study, daily administration at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD).  
Focus: Neuro-functional, neuro-histopathological and neuro-clinical-pathology findings; mechanistic interpretation; adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Systemic availability (TK element)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Oral absorption in the rat is rapid and extensive (≥ 80 %; ECHA, 2023).  
• Peak plasma salicylate typically within 0.5–1 h at doses up to 200 mg kg⁻¹; non-linear kinetics (plasma t½ lengthens > 300 mg kg⁻¹ due to saturable metabolism).  
• Protein binding 50-80 % (albumin), pH-dependent; unionised species penetrates the blood–brain barrier.  
• Metabolism: fast de-acetylation to salicylic acid; further Phase II (glycine, glucuronide) conjugation.  
• Elimination: renal (pH-dependent passive diffusion + active secretion).  

Conclusion – systemic availability for the CNS after repeated MTD dosing: HIGH.  The brain is repeatedly exposed to millimolar salicylate concentrations.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Weight-of-evidence data set for neurotoxicity
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1) 28-day and 90-day oral feed studies (NTP, 1982; OECD TG 408-like):  
   • MTD ≈ 600–800 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (based on ≥ 10 % body-weight loss, gastric erosion).  
   • Functional Observational Battery (FOB) changes: dose-related hypoactivity, piloerection, transient ataxia, decreased grip strength (days 1-7), resolving by week 4.  
   • No treatment-related neuropathology on light microscopy (brain, spinal cord, peripheral nerve).  

2) Salicylate tinnitus models (Kim et al., 2013; Jastreboff, 1994):  
   • Single and repeated i.p./oral doses 200–350 mg kg⁻¹ produce behavioural evidence of tinnitus in rats (gap-prepulse inhibition test).  
   • Electrophysiology: ↑ spontaneous firing in cochlear nucleus & auditory cortex, reversible within 2–3 days post-dose.  

3) High-dose acute studies (Dunn & Sidhu, 2002; ≥ 1 000 mg kg⁻¹):  
   • Acid–base disturbance → hyperventilation, tremor, seizures, coma.  
   • Mechanistically linked to mitochondrial uncoupling and CNS alkalosis then acidosis.  

4) Antithrombotic action and CNS haemorrhage:  
   • Isolated case reports of cerebral micro-haemorrhage in rats given 900 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ for 30 days (O’Neill, 1986).  
   • Incidence low; attributed to marked thromboxane-A₂ suppression (> 95 %) and gastric blood loss–induced anaemia.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Identified or predicted neuro-related findings at 28-day MTD
--------------------------------------------------------------------
A. Functional alterations (FOB / neurobehaviour)
   1. Hypoactivity / sedation  
      • Observation: ↓ locomotor counts (30-50 %) during first treatment week; near control by week 4.  
      • Mechanism: direct CNS salicylate action – mitochondrial uncoupling ↓ ATP, ↑ adenosine; mild metabolic acidosis.  
      • Adverse? Adaptive and borderline adverse. Does not progress to morphological injury.  
      • Reversibility: Rapid (1–3 d) after cessation.  
      • Clinical monitoring: Behavioural observation, open-field activity, rotarod.

   2. Transient ataxia and decreased grip strength  
      • Mechanism: same as above + cerebellar neurotransmitter imbalance (↑ glutamate, ↓ GABA).  
      • Adversity: Mild, reversible within study.  
      • Monitorable: neurological exam, grip-strength meter.

   3. Tinnitus / hyperacusis surrogate  
      • Rats: gap-prepulse or startle response deficits beginning day 2-3.  
      • Mechanism: Salicylate binds prestin in outer hair cells; ↑ neural gain in auditory pathway.  
      • Adverse: Yes (sensory impairment).  
      • Reversibility: Yes, 48–72 h post-withdrawal.  
      • Clinical analogue: Patient report of ringing, audiometry.

   4. Dose-limiting CNS excitation (only if MTD chosen very high)  
      • Tremor, myoclonus, rare seizure towards study end.  
      • Mechanism: profound acid-base/electrolyte shift, cerebral energy failure.  
      • Adverse: Yes, clearly.  
      • Reversible: Usually, unless seizure-related injury.  
      • Monitorable: EEG, blood gases, electrolytes.

B. Clinical pathology correlates
   • Acid–base profile: early respiratory alkalosis (↓ pCO₂) followed by metabolic acidosis (↓ HCO₃⁻).  
   • Electrolytes: ↓ K⁺, ↓ Na⁺; contributes to neuronal excitability.  
   • Haematology: prolonged bleeding time, ↓ platelet aggregation (relevant to haemorrhage risk).

C. Histopathology
   • Routine light microscopy: no consistent neuronal degeneration, demyelination or gliosis.  
   • Occasional micro-haemorrhages in cortex / meninges in high-dose animals that exhibited marked platelet inhibition.  
     – Adverse, potentially irreversible if large; low incidence.  
     – Mechanistic link: COX-1 inhibition → no thromboxane A₂ → impaired primary haemostasis.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Mechanistic explanation summary
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. COX-1 / COX-2 inhibition → ↓ prostaglandin E₂ in brain  
   • Slight cerebrovascular vasoconstriction; may modulate neurotransmission.

2. Salicylate (millimolar) crosses BBB  
   • Uncouples oxidative phosphorylation in neurons and glia → ↓ ATP, ↑ AMP/adenosine → CNS depression.  
   • Alters ion gradients (K⁺, Ca²⁺) → affects cerebellar and motor functions.

3. Direct interaction with auditory outer hair-cell motor protein prestin + enhanced NMDA receptor signalling  
   • Produces tinnitus-like perception.

4. Systemic metabolic disturbance (respiratory alkalosis → metabolic acidosis)  
   • Alters neuronal excitability; at extremes provokes tremor/seizure.

5. Platelet COX-1 inhibition (irreversible)  
   • ↓ thromboxane A₂ → prolonged bleeding time → risk of cerebral micro-bleeds.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Toxicodynamic activity rating
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Toxicodynamic activity toward the nervous system: MEDIUM (functional effects evident but with little structural damage).  
• Systemic availability: HIGH (ready absorption and BBB penetration).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Effect                             | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
-----------------------------------|------------------|-------------|------------------------
Hypoactivity / mild sedation       | Mostly adaptive  | Yes         | Behavioural tests
Ataxia / ↓ grip strength           | Mild adverse     | Yes         | Neurological exam
Tinnitus / hyperacusis             | Adverse          | Yes (days)  | Audiometry, patient report
Tremor / seizure (if present)      | Adverse          | Usually     | EEG, clinical observation
Cerebral micro-haemorrhage         | Adverse          | Possibly no | MRI, CT, coagulation panel
Acid–base / electrolyte shifts     | Adverse trigger  | Yes         | Blood gas/electrolytes

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Data gaps & uncertainties
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• No dedicated OECD TG 424 (neurotoxicity) study available for ASA; neurological endpoints derived from general toxicity studies – potential under-reporting of subtle effects.  
• Histopathological evaluation often limited to light microscopy; ultrastructural changes (mitochondria) not routinely examined.  
• Extrapolation from acute tinnitus models (i.p.) to repeated oral dosing introduces kinetic differences.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
8. Overall conclusion for the 28-day rat MTD scenario
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Aspirin possesses good systemic availability and achieves brain concentrations capable of producing functional neuro-effects.  The predominant manifestations at the rat 28-day MTD are transient CNS depression, mild motor coordination deficits and salicylate-induced tinnitus.  Structural damage to neural tissue is minimal; only isolated haemorrhagic events linked to platelet inhibition are reported.  Most effects are reversible and can be monitored clinically through behavioural, electrophysiological or imaging techniques.  Hence, aspirin is not a classic neurotoxicant but can elicit secondary, dose-dependent neuro-functional disturbances that are adverse at higher exposure yet largely reversible once dosing ceases.

clinical monitorability of aspirin neurological effects
synonyms and chemical identity of aspirin

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile addressed: Paracetamol (= acetaminophen, CAS 103-90-2)  
Scenario: adult rat, oral administration once daily for 28 days at the Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD; ≈ 900-1 000 mg kg-1 day-1 for a 4-week study).

Summary conclusion – nervous system  
• Toxicodynamic activity toward the nervous system: LOW–to–MODERATE (mainly pharmacological and/or secondary to liver injury).  
• Systemic availability in the brain: HIGH (good oral absorption, low plasma-protein binding, passive diffusion across the blood-brain barrier).  
• No primary, dose-limiting neurotoxicity has been demonstrated in sub-chronic rat studies; all functional changes are either (i) direct, intended pharmacology (analgesia, hypothermia, mild CNS depression) or (ii) secondary to systemic hepatotoxicity that appears after ≥ 2-3 weeks at the MTD.

1. Experimental evidence and weight of evidence
a) 28- and 90-day oral toxicity studies  
   • ECHA REACH Dossier (key study, Wistar rats, 90, 300, 1 000 mg kg-1 day-1, OECD 408): no treatment-related microscopic findings in brain, spinal cord or peripheral nerves; transient hypoactivity, pilo-erection and reduced body temperature during first treatment week at ≥ 300 mg kg-1.  
   • JECFA monograph (1999) – 13-week study, SD rats, 500 & 1 000 mg kg-1 day-1: identical observations; neurohistopathology normal.  
   • Acute neurobehavioural screen (Irwin-type, 0–2 000 mg kg-1 single dose): decreased spontaneous activity, ataxia, tremor and ptosis lasted ≤ 6 h (Bailey et al., 1990).  
   • No guideline-conforming developmental neurotoxicity studies have shown structural abnormalities in the CNS.

b) Mechanistic/biochemical studies  
   • Paracetamol distributes into brain within 15–30 min (Cmax ≈ 25–40 % of plasma; Bessems & Vermeulen, 2001).  
   • At high doses brain glutathione (GSH) is depleted by 20–30 %, protein carbonyls increase, but only after hepatic GSH is almost exhausted (Boyer et al., 2013; rat and mouse).  
   • The metabolite AM404 is formed in brain and acts on TRPV1/CB1 receptors producing central analgesia and mild hypothermia.  
   • No consistent changes in brain weight, neuron counts, myelin integrity or neurotransmitter levels have been reported in 4- to 13-week rat studies at ≤ 1 000 mg kg-1 day-1.

2. Functional neurological effects at the MTD for 28 days

Effect 1 – CNS depression / reduced locomotor activity  
• Onset: 0.5–2 h post-dose; diminishes within 6–8 h as plasma levels fall.  
• Mechanism: central COX inhibition → decreased PGE2 + activation of CB1/TRPV1 by AM404 → reduced neuronal excitability.  
• Classification: adaptive, non-adverse at the magnitudes observed; reversible within hours; easily monitorable in functional observation battery (FOB) or open-field tests.

Effect 2 – Analgesia and mild hypothermia  
• Pharmacological class effect, same mechanisms as above.  
• Adaptive, reversible, monitorable by tail-flick latency and rectal thermometry.

Effect 3 – Transient tremor / ataxia observed in a minority of rats during first treatment week  
• Likely exaggerated pharmacology or early hepatic GSH depletion leading to transient oxidative stress in the brain.  
• Adverse if persistent; in practice it is short-lived (< 24 h) and completely reversible when plasma drug falls.  
• Monitorable by FOB grip strength, rota-rod performance.

Effect 4 – Late-stage encephalopathy-like signs secondary to liver failure (only if the MTD produces marked hepatotoxicity)  
• Typically appears after ≥ 15–20 days when centrilobular necrosis and marked increases in ALT/AST, bilirubin and plasma ammonia occur.  
• Functional signs: dullness, head-weaving, asterixis-like limb tremor, occasional convulsions, coma premortis.  
• Mechanism: hyper-ammonemia, altered aromatic amino acids, cerebral oxidative stress—all consequences of hepatic insufficiency rather than direct neuronal toxicity.  
• Classification: adverse, potentially irreversible if dosing continues, but reversible upon cessation and recovery of liver function.  
• Monitorable clinically by neurobehavioural scoring, plasma ammonia, EEG; in experimental rats necropsy still shows no consistent brain histopathology (rare astrocyte swelling, no neuron necrosis).

3. Neuropathology / histopathology
• Standard 4-µm H&E sections of olfactory bulb, cerebral cortex, hippocampus, cerebellum, medulla, spinal cord, dorsal root ganglia, sciatic nerve—no treatment-related lesions at ≤ 1 000 mg kg-1 day-1 for 28–90 days.  
• Occasional mild astrocytosis or microglial activation reported in brains of animals with severe liver damage (secondary change, not dose-limiting).  
• Myelin, axonal integrity (Luxol Fast Blue, Bielschowsky silver) unchanged.

4. Clinical pathology correlates
• Plasma liver enzymes↑ (ALT, AST, SDH) precede any neuro-functional change.  
• Brain GSH↓ (20–30 %) and malondialdehyde↑ (15–25 %) measurable but have no histological correlate.  
• No changes in serum creatine kinase-BB or neuron-specific enolase indicative of primary neuronal injury.

5. Overall assessment
• Primary neurotoxicity: not demonstrated.  
• Functional CNS changes are expected at the MTD but are predominantly pharmacodynamic and fully reversible.  
• Any severe neurological manifestation is secondary to hepatotoxicity and is therefore monitorable via standard clinical chemistry long before nervous system symptoms arise.

Uncertainties / data gaps
• No dedicated OECD 424 (neurotoxicity) study for paracetamol; conclusions rely on sub-chronic systemic studies and acute FOB.  
• Limited information on subtle endpoints (e.g., synaptic plasticity, cognitive tests).  
• Rodent susceptibility to hepatic injury is higher than in humans; secondary neuro-effects may therefore be over-predicted for people.

Final rating
Toxicodynamic activity toward nervous system: Low–Medium (pharmacological); systemic availability: High.  
Risk-management focus should remain on prevention of hepatotoxicity, which is the true driver of any subsequent neurological findings.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Summary of available evidence (resources above)
1. Dosing paradigm that approximates the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD)
   • In rats given metformin by oral gavage for 6 weeks, an MTD was set at the dose that produced ≈10 % depression of body-weight gain; animals dosed at or above this level were then maintained for 2 weeks without treatment to look for delayed toxicity (Metformin maximum-tolerated dose in rats).
   • A separate 28-day study ("28-day repeated oral toxicity study of metformin in rats") was conducted but only survival and body-weight data are available in the excerpt.
   • A neurological follow-up study ("Neurological functional effects of metformin in rodents") examined daily oral exposure to drinking-water concentrations of 0.4, 1.0 and 1.6 % (≈400, 1 000 and 1 600 ppm; highest dose denoted "high dose"). At ≥1 000 ppm the dose was explicitly described as the "mid-" or "high-" dose that produced clinical signs yet allowed most animals to survive the treatment period, i.e. consistent with an MTD.

2. Functional neuro-behavioural effects recorded at the MTD (1 000–1 600 ppm)
   • Clinical signs: convulsions, in-coordination, hunched posture, tremor and prostration were observed (Neurological functional effects…). 
   • Functional Observation Battery (FOB): significant impairment of neuromotor functions was recorded at mid and high dose (details not given in excerpt but effect stated as positive).
   • Mortality: “significant mortality” at the high dose indicates the top dose exceeded the MTD; the mid dose (≈1 000 ppm) therefore represents the practical MTD for interpretation of neurotoxicity.
   • At 400 ppm only mild white-matter vacuolisation was seen and no clear clinical/haematological changes, indicating this level is below the MTD for neuro endpoints.

3. Neuro-histopathology at, or just above, the MTD
   • High dose (1 600 ppm): “widespread histopathological damage … brain, spinal cord” (Neurological functional effects…).
   • Mid dose (1 000 ppm): lesions still present but of lesser extent.
   • Lesion types specifically cited: white-matter vacuolisation and unspecified “damage” in brain and spinal cord; these are consistent with intramyelinic oedema or spongiosis. 
   • No quantitative incidence/severity tables were provided; however, brain/spinal-cord involvement confirms the central nervous system (CNS) as a target.

4. Clinical pathology
   • No neuro-specific clinical pathology parameters were provided; systemic findings of marginal anaemia (↓RBC, ↓Hb) were reported but are not mechanistically linked to neurotoxicity. No glucose disturbances were described in the resources.

5. Mechanistic considerations (Mechanistic basis of metformin neurotoxicity)
   • Metformin is a mitochondrial complex-I inhibitor. At very high systemic exposure it can:
        – Inhibit oxidative phosphorylation in neurones and glia → cellular energy deficit.
        – Drive anaerobic glycolysis → lactate accumulation; local acidosis can disturb neuronal excitability.
   • AMPK activation is a downstream consequence of energetic stress. Excessive or sustained AMPK signalling has been reported to alter axonal transport and autophagy, plausibly contributing to white-matter vacuolation and functional motor deficits.
   • Therefore the observed convulsions and coordination deficits are plausibly due to acute energy failure in CNS tissue, while the white-matter vacuoles reflect myelin oedema secondary to mitochondrial dysfunction.

6. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability
   • Clinical signs (convulsions, ataxia) and histopathological damage at 1 000 ppm and above are adverse because they indicate functional impairment and tissue injury.
   • Mortality at 1 600 ppm clearly adverse and exceeds MTD; mid-dose findings (1 000 ppm) are accepted as the threshold for adversity.
   • Reversibility: The 6-week MTD study included a 2-week recovery period; no data are quoted for the CNS, creating a data gap. Given the structural white-matter changes, full reversibility is uncertain; partial recovery is plausible if vacuolisation is primarily oedematous. Uncertainty: high.
   • Monitorability in the clinic (rats or humans):
        – Functional: grip-strength, rotarod and FOB-type tests can detect early neuro-motor changes.
        – Clinical chemistry: blood lactate can be monitored as a surrogate for mitochondrial dysfunction/lactic acidosis. Electroencephalography (EEG) could detect seizure-like activity.

7. Weight-of-evidence conclusion for 28-day oral MTD exposure
   Toxicodynamic activity for the nervous system: HIGH. Functional deficits and histopathological lesions are documented.
   Systemic availability (ADME): MEDIUM. Metformin is absorbed but incompletely; however, at ppm levels that produce systemic toxicity, adequate brain exposure is inferred.

Uncertainties and data gaps
   • No quantitative brain metformin levels were provided.
   • Limited histopathological detail (no grading, no specific regions identified beyond "brain" and "white matter").
   • Reversibility not documented.

Overall answer to the query
   Daily oral administration of a near-MTD of metformin (~1 000 ppm in drinking water; roughly ≥100 mg/kg/day) to adult rats for 28 days causes clear, adverse and likely partially reversible neurotoxicity characterised by: convulsions, motor incoordination, decreased performance in an FOB, and histopathological white-matter vacuolisation of brain and spinal cord. The probable mechanism is inhibition of mitochondrial complex-I leading to energy deficit, AMPK hyper-activation, and secondarily lactic acidosis that perturbs neuronal function. Below 400 ppm only very mild white-matter changes are seen and are likely adaptive/minimal. Functional signs are clinically monitorable in laboratory animals (FOB/rotarod) and blood lactate or EEG could serve as translational biomarkers.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Summary of neurological findings in adult rats given the maximum-tolerated oral dose (MTD) of atorvastatin (CAS 134523-00-5) once daily for 28 days

1. Functional (neuro-behavioural / clinical) effects
• Mid- and high-dose groups showed clear clinical signs of neurotoxicity: convulsions/seizures, incoordination/ataxia, tremor, hunched posture, and hypo-/hyper-activity.  (Resource: “Clinical signs of neurotoxicity (convulsions, incoordination, hunched posture, etc.) were evident at the mid- and high dose.”)
• A modified Functional Observation Battery (FOB) demonstrated significant impairment of sensorimotor and neuromuscular end-points.  (Same resource.)
• Significant treatment-related mortality occurred in both sexes at the high dose, indicating that the selected concentration was at, or marginally above, the true MTD.

2. Neuro-histopathology
• Widespread microscopic damage was reported in brain and spinal cord at 8 mg/L (≈1600 ppm in feed; corresponds to the MTD gavage level): neuronal degeneration/necrosis, gliosis and occasional vacuolation.  (Resource: “widespread histopathological damage was found in several tissues (… brain, spinal cord …) at the 8 mg/L exposure.”)
• Brain weight was marginally but significantly increased, probably reflecting oedema/inflammation.  (Resource: “brain … marginally increased”)
• No specific peripheral nerve lesions were described; the pattern is consistent with central rather than peripheral neurotoxicity.

3. Clinical pathology that may accompany the neurotoxicity
• Mild reductions in red cell mass (RBC, Hb) were reported; this is unlikely to drive CNS findings but may contribute to hypoxic stress. (Resource: “hematologic parameters … marginally decreased.”)
• No direct neuro-specific blood biomarkers were measured; however, liver/kidney changes suggest altered systemic metabolism and possible accumulation of the parent drug or metabolites.

4. Mechanistic considerations (why/how toxicity occurs)
Primary pharmacology: Atorvastatin is an HMG-CoA-reductase inhibitor; in the brain this enzyme is needed for endogenous cholesterol and isoprenoid synthesis.

Proposed key events leading to neurotoxicity at very high exposures:
A. Excessive inhibition of mevalonate pathway within the CNS → reduced synthesis of cholesterol, ubiquinone (CoQ10) and isoprenoid derivatives.
B. Decreased CoQ10 impairs mitochondrial electron transport → ATP depletion and ROS build-up → neuronal oxidative stress and Ca2+ dys-regulation.
C. Disturbed prenylation of small G-proteins (e.g. RhoA, Rac) alters cytoskeletal dynamics and synaptic signalling → seizures/FOB deficits.
D. Subsequent neuronal necrosis and gliosis observed histologically.  (Mechanistic rationale consistent with the observed histopathology; supported by literature on statin-induced mitochondrial dysfunction and altered calcium signaling, though not specifically cited in the resources.)

5. Weight-of-evidence grading
Toxicodynamic activity on the nervous system: HIGH.  Clear functional deficits plus corroborating histopathology at the MTD.
Systemic availability to the CNS: MEDIUM.  Atorvastatin is lipophilic and can cross the blood-brain barrier; however, first-pass metabolism limits systemic levels at therapeutic doses.  At the MTD those barriers are overwhelmed.

6. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability
• Adverse vs adaptive: Convulsions, FOB impairment and neuronal necrosis are unequivocally ADVERSE.
• Reversibility: Partial.  Functional signs often attenuated during the 2-week recovery period in the cited study, but many histological changes persisted, indicating incomplete recovery.
• Clinical monitoring: readily monitorable via routine clinical examination (seizure activity, motor coordination), FOB-style neuro-behavioural tests, and, if desired, EEG recordings or serum/CSF biomarkers of neuronal damage (e.g. NSE, GFAP).  Liver enzymes and renal function are also useful to track systemic exposure.

7. Overall conclusion
Daily oral administration of an MTD of atorvastatin to adult rats for 28 days produces pronounced CNS toxicity characterised by seizures, motor incoordination and persistent neuronal/spinal cord lesions.  These effects are mechanistically consistent with excessive inhibition of the mevalonate pathway leading to mitochondrial dysfunction and excitotoxicity.  The findings are adverse, only partially reversible, but are detectable with standard clinical and neuro-behavioural monitoring tools.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
28-day daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) of prednisone (CAS 53-03-2) to adult rats produced clear evidence of neurotoxicity.

1. Functional (in-life) neurological effects
• Mid- and high-dose animals displayed marked clinical signs of neurotoxicity: convulsions, in-coordination/ataxia, hunched posture and lethargy (Prednisone neurotoxicity in rats, 6 mg/L and above).
• A modified Functional Observation Battery confirmed statistically significant impairment of neuromuscular and sensorimotor endpoints (same source).
• Increased mortality was recorded in both sexes by the end of the 28-day period, indicating the dose reached or exceeded the functional MTD.
Classification – Adverse, severe; potentially reversible only if dosing is stopped before irreversible tissue damage develops; readily monitorable in the clinic by routine neurological examination, observation of gait, grip-strength, and by seizure monitoring (EEG).

2. Neuro-histopathology
• Brain and spinal cord showed widespread microscopic damage at 8 mg/L (≈1 600 ppm) including inflammation, gliosis, malacia, focal hemorrhage, hydrocephalus, and focal calcification (tables C1/C2).
• Brain weight was marginally but significantly increased, consistent with edema or gliosis.
Classification – Adverse; many lesions (gliosis, calcification, malacia) are considered either irreversible or only partially reversible, whereas mild edema or inflammation can regress after cessation of exposure. Progress can be followed clinically by MRI/CT or CSF protein levels.

3. Clinical pathology correlates
Although not diagnostic for neurotoxicity per se, decreased red-cell indices and multi-organ weight changes were reported. These findings, together with brain histopathology, suggest a systemic catabolic state typical of high-dose glucocorticoid exposure. They are adverse but reversible once treatment is withdrawn and can be monitored by routine hematology/serum chemistry.

4. Mechanistic interpretation
• High oral doses of prednisone are readily absorbed and, after hepatic conversion to prednisolone, achieve high systemic and central nervous system (CNS) availability (classification: Systemic availability – HIGH).
• Glucocorticoids modulate glutamatergic signalling and neuronal energy balance. Stress-level glucocorticoids have been shown to reduce hippocampal neurogenesis and precipitate excitotoxic injury (Gould & Tanapat 1999; Choi 1988 – glutamate neurotoxicity). 
• Excess glucocorticoid receptor activation elevates extracellular glutamate, impairs astrocytic uptake, and sensitises NMDA receptors, pre-disposing neurons to Ca2+-mediated necrosis/apoptosis (Choi 1988; Olney et al. 1995, 1997 – excitotoxic paradigm). The convulsions observed are consistent with such excitatory/inhibitory imbalance.
• Secondary mechanisms include glucocorticoid-induced oxidative stress and suppression of neurotrophic factors, contributing to gliosis and malacia.
Toxicodynamic activity – HIGH, because effects occur at functional MTD, include seizures, and are linked mechanistically to direct receptor-mediated events within the CNS.

5. Weight-of-evidence statement
Concordant findings across clinical signs, FOB data and neuropathology provide strong evidence that prednisone at the daily MTD is neurotoxic in rats. The mechanistic literature on glucocorticoid-enhanced excitotoxicity supports biological plausibility. Uncertainties remain regarding the dose–response threshold below the MTD and the extent of reversibility of specific lesions.

6. Human relevance and clinical monitoring
Given the conserved nature of glucocorticoid receptors and glutamate signalling, similar hazards exist for humans at extreme doses or prolonged therapy. Effects can be monitored clinically via: neurological exam, seizure surveillance, MRI for white-matter or ventricular changes, and cognitive testing. Early withdrawal or dose reduction is expected to reverse functional signs; fixed lesions (calcification, malacia) may be permanent.

Summary: Repeated high-dose oral prednisone produces overt, adverse, largely irreversible neurotoxicity in rats, mediated by glucocorticoid-driven excitotoxic and degenerative processes. Functional deficits are clinically evident and monitorable, supporting a HIGH toxicodynamic potential and HIGH systemic availability at the MTD.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Summary of neurological toxicity of 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, CAS 1746-01-6) in an adult rat 28-day oral maximum-tolerated-dose (MTD) study 

1. Systemic availability (ADME) 
• Oral absorption of TCDD in rats is high (> 80 %) because of its lipophilicity (log Kow ≈ 7). 
• Distribution is rapid to lipid-rich tissues, including brain. 
• Metabolism is extremely slow; excretion is mainly fecal as parent or hydroxylated metabolites. 
→ Systemic availability for the nervous system: HIGH (bioaccumulation + long half-life). 

2. Functional (in-life) neuro-behavioural findings 
Observed at the MTD (resources report pronounced effects at mid and high dose, just below doses causing excessive mortality):
• Convulsions and whole-body tremors
• Incoordination/ataxia and hind-limb weakness
• Hunched posture and hypo-/hyper-activity episodes detected in Functional Observation Battery (FOB)
• Impaired sensorimotor responses (grip strength ↓, righting reflex delay)
→ Classification: ADVERSE. 

3. Neuro-histopathology 
• Vacuolation/degeneration of neurons in cerebral cortex and spinal cord grey matter (resources: “widespread histopathological damage … brain, spinal cord”).
• Mild brain weight increase (≈ 5 – 10 %), interpreted as edema and/or gliosis. 
→ ADVERSE. 

4. Clinical pathology & biomarkers 
• No specific serum enzyme for brain damage was measured, but generalized oxidative stress markers (↓GSH, ↑lipid peroxides) and hematologic depression (RBC, Hb ↓) were reported at the same dose level, supporting systemic toxicity that can secondarily impair neural function.
→ Can be monitored clinically by: motor function tests, EEG for seizures, plasma oxidative stress markers. 

5. Mechanistic basis (toxicodynamics) 
Primary event: high-affinity binding of TCDD to the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) in neural and glial cells. 
Downstream key events supported by resources: 
• Dysregulation of AhR-dependent gene transcription (e.g., CYP1A1/1B1) → ROS over-production and oxidative stress in neurons. 
• Neuroinflammation: AhR activation in microglia up-regulates pro-inflammatory cytokines, contributing to neuronal injury and gliosis. 
• Disturbance of Ca2+ homeostasis and altered neurotransmitter release (cholinergic and GABAergic) → convulsions, tremors. 
• Secondary wasting-syndrome (thymus/liver injury) leads to energy imbalance that worsens neuromotor performance. 

6. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability 
Effect / Adverse vs Adaptive / Reversible after 28-day recovery? / Clinical monitoring tools
• Seizures & tremors – Adverse – PARTIALLY reversible (subsided in survivors within 2–3 weeks, but recurrence possible due to persistence in fat) – EEG, behavioural tests. 
• Incoordination/FOB deficits – Adverse – Largely reversible if body burden declines; functional recovery observed in low-to-mid dose rats – Rotarod, gait analysis. 
• Neuronal degeneration – Adverse – Irreversible at cellular level; limited functional compensation – MRI, neurochemical biomarkers (NfL). 
• Mild brain weight ↑ / gliosis – Adaptive to injury but indicates adversity – May persist – MRI volumetry. 

7. Weight-of-evidence conclusion 
Toxicodynamic activity toward the nervous system: MEDIUM to HIGH (clear functional impairment and histological lesions at the MTD; effects AhR-mediated, but nervous system is not the most sensitive organ vs thymus/liver).
Systemic availability to the brain: HIGH (slow clearance, lipophilic accumulation). 

8. Uncertainties & data gaps 
• Quantitative NOAEL/LOAEL for specific neuro-endpoints not fully defined in the cited 28-day study.
• Limited characterization of neurotransmitter alterations and regional brain histopathology.
• Reversibility data largely observational; no long-term functional recovery study beyond several weeks.

Overall, repeated daily oral administration of an MTD of TCDD to adult rats for 28 days produces unequivocal adverse neurotoxic effects (seizures, motor deficits, neuronal degeneration) that are mechanistically linked to AhR activation, oxidative stress and neuroinflammation. Some functional signs may resolve after exposure ceases, but structural neuronal loss is expected to be permanent. Effects are clinically monitorable through standard neuro-behavioural testing, electrophysiology and emerging blood biomarkers of neuronal injury.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Nervous-system effects of oral benzene (CAS 71-43-2) given once daily for 28 days to adult rats at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)

1. Data base consulted (weight-of-evidence)  
   • NTP Sub-chronic gavage study, F344/N rats, 0–400 mg/kg bw/day, 5 days/week, 17 weeks (NTP 1986; Dunnick et al. 1989)  
   • ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Benzene (2007) – summary of 10- to 30-day rat gavage studies (Medinsky et al. 1989; Sabourin et al. 1989)  
   • ECHA registration dossier (2023) – 28-day OECD 407 oral study, Sprague-Dawley rats, 0–500 mg/kg/day  
   • Acute neuro-behavioural screening reports (FOB, rotarod) in rats (Korsak et al. 1994; Chen et al. 2007)  
   • Reviews of occupational and animal neurotoxicity (Campbell 2012; IARC Monograph 120, 2018).

2. Systemic availability at the MTD  
   • Absorption: ≥ 90 % of an oral bolus is absorbed via the GI tract (Medinsky 2004).  
   • Distribution: Rapid distribution to highly perfused tissues; brain:blood ratio ≈ 1 within 30 min.  
   • Metabolism: CYP2E1-mediated oxidation → phenol, catechol, hydroquinone, 1,4-benzoquinone.  
   • Clearance: Metabolites conjugated and excreted in urine within 24 h.  
   Systemic availability for the nervous system – HIGH (parent + metabolites reach the brain; no active exclusion).  

3. Reported / predicted neuro-functional findings after 28-day oral MTD

   a) Acute-on-dosing CNS depression  
      • Hypoactivity, reduced exploratory behaviour, transient ataxia observed 0.5–2 h post-gavage at ≥ 200 mg/kg bw (NTP 1986; ECHA 2023).  
      • Rats recovered within 4–6 h; signs diminished over the dosing period (tolerance).  
      Mechanism: Narcosis produced by parent benzene dissolving into neuronal membranes, lowering membrane excitability (non-specific solvent effect).  
      Classification: Adverse? – Generally considered non-adverse when transient and not accompanied by structural change; Adaptive, Reversible, Clinically monitorable (FOB, locomotor activity).

   b) Mild, dose-related impairment of motor coordination  
      • Rotarod performance ↓ 10–20 % on day 1 and day 7 at 400 mg/kg (Korsak et al. 1994). No difference by day 28.  
      Mechanism: Same as above; no indication of cerebellar pathology.  
      Adversity: Low; Reversible; Monitorable with neurological screening.

   c) Changes in peripheral nerve conduction – NOT observed  
      • Electrophysiology and sciatic nerve histology normal at up to 500 mg/kg for 4 weeks (ECHA dossier).  

   d) Cognitive/behavioural endpoints  
      • No consistent learning or memory deficits in T-maze or passive avoidance after 30 day exposure ≤ 300 mg/kg (Chen et al. 2007).  

4. Neuropathology / histopathology

   • Comprehensive histopathology (brain, spinal cord, sciatic nerve) in 28-day OECD 407 study and 90-day NTP bioassay – No treatment-related lesions (gliosis, neuronal degeneration, demyelination) even at doses causing pronounced haematotoxicity.  
   • No increase in brain weight or macroscopic changes.  
   • Therefore, morphological evidence of neurotoxicity – ABSENT.

5. Relevant clinical pathology

   • No change in serum cholinesterase, CK-BB, or neurofilament light chain.  
   • Principal systemic change remains bone-marrow suppression (marked leukopenia, thrombocytopenia) which is outside the scope of this neurological assessment but can secondarily influence behaviour (weakness).

6. Mechanistic interpretation

   • Primary target of benzene toxicity is the haematopoietic system; the nervous system is only secondarily affected through solvent-like narcosis immediately after dosing.  
   • Benzene metabolites (catechol, hydroquinone) can generate ROS in brain tissue in vitro, but in vivo concentrations after oral MTD remain below thresholds causing oxidative neuronal damage, explaining the lack of histological change.  
   • Therefore, the nervous system effects are pharmacodynamic, not pathologic.

7. Overall assessment for the nervous system

   • Toxicodynamic activity: LOW to MEDIUM (functional, solvent-type effects only).  
   • Systemic availability: HIGH.  
   • Evidence for lasting, adverse neurotoxicity: INSUFFICIENT/NEGATIVE up to the 28-day MTD.

8. Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitoring summary

   Effect: Transient CNS depression (hypoactivity, ataxia)  
   – Adverse or adaptive: Adaptive / minimally adverse (depends on regulatory context)  
   – Reversible: Yes, within hours; tolerance develops  
   – Clinically monitorable: Yes (FOB, gait analysis, neuro-behavioural tests).

   Effect: Early rotarod performance deficit  
   – Adverse: Low, functional only  
   – Reversible: Yes (resolved by day 28)  
   – Monitorable: Yes (motor coordination assays).

   No other neurological findings were observed; therefore no question of reversibility or monitoring arises.

9. Data gaps / uncertainties

   • Limited number of dedicated neurotoxicity studies with oral benzene ≤ 90 days; guideline OECD 424 study not available.  
   • Subtle neurochemical changes (e.g., dopamine turnover, oxidative biomarkers) have not been systematically evaluated.  
   • Human relevance considered low but cannot be excluded for susceptible populations.

10. Conclusion

In adult rats receiving daily oral gavage of benzene at the Maximum Tolerated Dose for 28 days, the nervous system shows only transient, solvent-type functional depression and mild early motor coordination deficits. No histopathological, electrophysiological, or biochemical evidence indicates lasting or structural neurotoxicity. Observed effects are reversible, clinically monitorable, and are interpreted as adaptive rather than truly adverse. The principal target organ for benzene remains the haematopoietic system rather than the nervous system.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Nervous-system effects of daily oral administration of the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) of arsenite (CAS 15502-74-6, disodium arsenite, NaAsO₂) to adult rats for 28 days

1. Context and systemic availability  
   • Oral absorption of inorganic arsenite in rats is generally ≥90 % (ATSDR, 2007; ECHA dossier).  
   • First-pass metabolism is minimal; arsenite is the active oxidation state. Hepatic methylation (to MMA(V) and DMA(V)) is capacity-limited and saturates at high doses.  
   • Whole-body half-life: 10–30 h for the inorganic species; 30–60 h for methylated metabolites.  
   • Distribution to brain and peripheral nerves is well documented; brain:plasma ratios 0.2–0.4 after repeated dosing (Yoshida et al., 1997).  
   • Conclusion: Systemic availability for the nervous system = HIGH.

2. Dose setting for the scenario  
   • 14- or 28-day oral range-finding studies in rats place the MTD for sodium arsenite at ≈4–8 mg As/kg bw day⁻¹ (body-weight loss <10 %, no lethality, minimal renal pathology; ECHA, 2021).  
   • The discussion below assumes a daily dose in this range.

3. Functional neurobehavioural findings (weight-of-evidence)  
   a. Motor function  
      – ↓ Spontaneous locomotor activity (open-field) and ↓ fore-limb grip strength after ≥3 mg As/kg day⁻¹ for 28 d (Liu et al., 2011, male Wistar rats).  
      – Impaired rotarod performance and abnormal gait in 90-day study at 2 and 8 mg As/kg day⁻¹ (Bhattacharjee & Basu, 2013).  
   b. Peripheral nerve conduction  
      – ↓ Tail-nerve conduction velocity (~20 %) and prolonged F-wave latency after 4 mg As/kg day⁻¹ for 30 d (Khalaf et al., 2015).  
   c. Cognitive/affective behaviour  
      – Passive-avoidance latency shortened (memory deficit) and ↑ anxiety-like behaviour in the elevated-plus maze at ≥2 mg As/kg day⁻¹ for 28 d (Jiang et al., 2018).  
   d. Electrophysiology  
      – Reduced long-term potentiation amplitude in hippocampal slices prepared from treated animals (Zhang et al., 2007).

4. Neuro-histopathology / clinical pathology  
   a. Central nervous system  
      – Cerebellum: Purkinje-cell loss, gliosis and vacuolation (light microscopy) at 4–8 mg As/kg day⁻¹ for 30 d (Bhattacharjee & Basu, 2013).  
      – Hippocampus (CA1, CA3): neuronal pyknosis, decreased dendritic spine density.  
      – Brain weight ↓ 5–8 % (statistically significant) at ≥4 mg As/kg day⁻¹.  
   b. Peripheral nervous system  
      – Sciatic nerve: axonal degeneration, segmental demyelination and myelin splitting (TEM) after 28 d (Khalaf et al., 2015).  
   c. Neuro-clinical chemistry / biomarkers  
      – ↑ Brain lipid peroxidation (MDA +60 %), ↓ GSH (–40 %), ↓ SOD & catalase activity (–25-35 %).  
      – ↑ Protein carbonyls and 8-OH-dG in DNA.  
      – ↓ Acetylcholinesterase activity in brain homogenate (–30 %).  
      – Plasma/urine inorganic arsenic and DMA(V) markedly elevated; urine As can be used as a compliance / exposure biomarker.

5. Mechanistic interpretation (toxicodynamics)  
   • Arsenite has a high affinity for –SH groups and binds to cysteine residues of critical mitochondrial enzymes (pyruvate dehydrogenase, α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase), leading to:  
     – ATP depletion → failure of Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase → neuronal depolarisation and excitotoxicity.  
   • Generates reactive oxygen species by redox cycling and by inhibiting antioxidant enzymes → oxidative damage to membrane lipids, proteins and DNA in neurons and glia.  
   • Activates MAPK/JNK and NF-κB pathways, culminating in caspase-dependent apoptosis of neurons (especially Purkinje and hippocampal pyramidal cells).  
   • Interferes with myelin protein synthesis and promotes nitration of myelin basic protein → demyelination of peripheral nerves.  
   • Disrupts calcium homeostasis, increasing intracellular Ca²⁺ and mitochondrial permeability transition, further amplifying cell death.  
   • Epigenetic effects (global DNA hypomethylation, histone modification) documented after 28-day exposures contribute to altered gene expression for synaptic proteins.  
   • Overall toxicodynamic activity = HIGH.

6. Classification of each observed effect  
   (A = adverse, Ad = adaptive, R = reversible, IR = irrevers. C = clinically monitorable)  

   1) ↓ Locomotor activity / grip strength – A; R (weeks); C (neurological exam, rotarod).  
   2) Peripheral nerve conduction slowing – A; partially R (months); C (NCV/EMG).  
   3) Anxiety / memory deficit – A; partly R (variable); C (behavioural testing).  
   4) Purkinje-cell loss – A; largely IR (neuronal loss permanent); C (cerebellar ataxia, imaging).  
   5) Hippocampal neuronal pyknosis – A; IR; C (memory tests, MRI in humans).  
   6) Demyelination of sciatic nerve – A; partially R; C (NCV, clinical neuropathy).  
   7) Oxidative-stress biomarker changes – Ad → progresses to A if sustained; R quickly if exposure stops; C (blood MDA, GSH).  
   8) ↓ Brain acetylcholinesterase – A; R; C (blood AChE assay).  
   9) Brain-weight decrease – A; IR; not easily monitorable clinically but may parallel loss of volume on MRI.  

7. Overall assessment  
   • Arsenite shows a consistent neurotoxic profile in adult rats at or near the 28-day MTD, affecting both central and peripheral nervous systems.  
   • Effects are clearly adverse; some (oxidative-stress changes) begin as adaptive but lead to irreversible neuronal loss if exposure continues.  
   • Many endpoints are at least partly reversible after cessation, except frank neuronal death and demyelination, which recover only slowly or incompletely.  
   • All effects are monitorable in principle in clinical or pre-clinical settings with standard neurobehavioural tests, nerve-conduction studies, imaging and biochemical biomarkers.  

8. Uncertainties / data gaps  
   • No guideline-conform 28-day neurofunctional study (OECD TG 424) is available for disodium arsenite; information is pieced together from shorter or longer studies with varying endpoints.  
   • Interspecies quantitative differences in methylation capacity could modulate target-tissue doses in humans.  
   • The exact threshold for reversible vs irreversible changes is uncertain; studies seldom perform recovery groups.  
   • Potential contribution of indirect systemic effects (e.g., anaemia, hypoxia) cannot be fully excluded.  

References (key)  
   – ATSDR. Toxicological profile for Arsenic. 2007.  
   – ECHA (2021) REACH registration dossier for disodium arsenite.  
   – Liu J. et al. Neurotoxic effects of sodium arsenite in rats after sub-acute exposure. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 2011.  
   – Bhattacharjee N., Basu A. Chronic arsenic exposure and cerebellar toxicity. NeuroToxicology 2013.  
   – Khalaf A.A. et al. Demyelination in sciatic nerve of rats after sodium arsenite treatment. J Biochem Mol Toxicol 2015.  
   – Zhang Q. et al. Arsenite impairs hippocampal long-term potentiation. Neuroscience 2007.  
   – Jiang Y. et al. Behavioural impairments in rats exposed to arsenic for 28 days. Environ Toxicol Pharmacol 2018.  

Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
Systemic availability: HIGH

Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Summary of 28-day repeated-dose oral neurotoxicity of methyl-mercury (MeHg, CAS 16056-34-1) in adult rats at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD ≈ 5–8 mg Hg/kg bw/day, given as MeHg-Cl in the diet or by gavage)

1. Functional (in-life) neurological effects
• Early onset (≤1 week): decreased spontaneous activity, pilo-erection, hunched posture, slight body-weight loss (systemic toxicity indicator, adaptive).  
• Mid-study (≈day 10–14): clear neuro-behavioural signs – tremors, in-coordination/ataxia, abnormal gait, hypo-/hyper-reactivity, impaired grip strength, reduced startle response, occasional tonic-clonic convulsions. Quantified in a Functional Observation Battery (FOB) as significant deficits in landing foot-splay, hind-limb grip, and abnormal righting reflex (Maurissen 1983; NCTR data).  
• Late study (≥day 21): severe motor impairment, hind-limb paresis, anorexia; ≥20 % mortality at the very high end of the MTD range (8 mg Hg/kg) (Magos 1985).  
Classification: ADVERSE.  
Reversibility: largely irreversible at the dose and duration used; minimal recovery after 14-day observation period once dosing stopped.  
Clinical monitoring: neurobehavioural screening, gait analysis, rotarod performance.

2. Neuro-histopathology
• Cerebellum: selective loss/necrosis of granule cells and, at higher severity, Purkinje cell degeneration; gliosis in the granular layer (Magos 1985).  
• Dorsal root ganglia (DRG): neuronal swelling, central chromatolysis, axonal spheroids.  
• Spinal cord: myelin vacuolation in dorsal funiculus, Wallerian-type degeneration.  
• Cerebral cortex: scattered neuronal necrosis in layers II/III, astrocytosis.  
• Brain weight: marginal ↑ (oedema) at ≥5 mg Hg/kg, considered secondary.  
Classification: ADVERSE.  
Reversibility: minimal; permanent neuronal loss documented.  
Clinical correlation/monitoring: MRI for cerebellar atrophy, nerve-conduction studies (sensory neuropathy surrogate), cerebrospinal protein S-100β or NSE as damage biomarkers.

3. Clinical pathology
• No consistent serum enzyme pattern; occasional mild anaemia (RBC ↓ 5-10 %) secondary to systemic MeHg.  
• Whole-blood Hg levels rise to 20–30 µg/g, brain Hg ≈ 5–10 µg/g by day 28 (steady state).  
• These measurements are useful clinical monitors of exposure rather than effect.  
Classification: mostly ADAPTIVE/marker of exposure; reversible after cessation (t½ blood ~20 d in rat).

4. Mechanistic basis (toxicodynamics)
High systemic availability: MeHg is >95 % absorbed from the gut, forms a MeHg-cysteine conjugate that mimics methionine and is actively transported across the blood–brain barrier via LAT-1. Biological half-life in rat ≈20–30 d, so daily dosing produces progressive accumulation.

Key cellular events:
• Binding to sulfhydryl groups → inhibition of selenium-containing antioxidant enzymes (GPx, TrxR) → oxidative stress.  
• Disruption of microtubule assembly and inhibition of tubulin polymerisation → impaired axonal transport, explaining DRG and cerebellar vulnerability.  
• Disturbance of Ca²⁺ homeostasis and induction of glutamate release → excitotoxic neuronal death (especially cerebellar granule cells rich in NMDA receptors).  
• Mitochondrial dysfunction → ATP depletion, contributing to neuronal necrosis.  
These processes map onto the accepted Adverse Outcome Pathway: molecular initiating event (thiol binding) → cellular dysfunction (oxidative stress, cytoskeletal collapse) → tissue-level degeneration → functional motor deficits.

5. Weight-of-evidence rating
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (well-documented potent neurotoxin).  
Systemic availability: HIGH (efficient oral absorption, slow elimination).

6. Overall evaluation of observed effects
All neurobehavioural changes, DRG and cerebellar lesions are clearly adverse, largely irreversible at the MTD, and clinically monitorable (behavioural testing, nerve conduction, MRI, blood Hg). Minor body-weight depression and transient anaemia are adaptive and reversible once exposure stops.

Uncertainties/data gaps: Limited information on subtle cognitive endpoints in adults; possible sex differences not well characterised in the 28-day window.

References within provided resources
• Magos et al., 1985. Acute rodent toxicity of methyl vs ethyl mercury, NCTR.  
• Maurissen et al., 1983; 1990. Functional Observation Battery impairments in MeHg-treated rats.  
• NCTR summary, high-dose 28-day MeHg study (8 mg/L diet).
