vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Vinyl chloride (VC, chloroethene; CAS 75-01-4) is best known for its hepatic carcinogenicity, but a consistent body of human, animal-inhalation and in-vitro evidence also shows that it can damage the nervous system after repeated exposure. Very few guideline-quality, 28-day oral studies are available; therefore the assessment below integrates  

• the limited oral studies that do exist,  
• the much larger inhalation data set in rats and mice (ECHA dossier, ATSDR 2020, Bolt 2016),  
• mechanistic data on the metabolites chloroethylene-oxide (CEO) and chloroacetaldehyde (CAA), and  
• general principles of neuro-toxicokinetics.  

A weight-of-evidence read-across from inhalation to the oral route is considered acceptable because:

1. VC is a small, highly lipophilic gas (logKow ≈ 2.4; Henry’s constant 0.91 atm·m³ mol⁻¹).  
2. Systemic (blood) concentrations drive toxicity, not local portal-of-entry effects.  
3. Inhalation and oral exposure yield the same reactive metabolites through the identical CYP2E1-mediated pathway in liver (and to a minor extent in brain).  

Uncertainties: Oral absorption kinetics in rodents are poorly quantified and first-pass hepatic extraction reduces parent VC reaching the brain, so external doses needed to reach the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) are higher than for inhalation. Nevertheless, the liver becomes saturated at high doses, allowing significant systemic parent VC and metabolite escape to extra-hepatic tissues, including nervous tissue.

-------------------------------------------------
1. Systemic availability (ADME) after oral gavage at the MTD
-------------------------------------------------
• Absorption: 40–70 % of an oral bolus is absorbed in rats (Hefner 1975, limited study).  
• Distribution: Rapid distribution to lipid-rich tissues; brain/blood ratio ≈ 1 within 30 min in inhalation studies, assumed similar orally once first-pass is saturated.  
• Metabolism: High-affinity, low-capacity CYP2E1 oxidation to CEO → CAA → 2-chloroethanol / 2-chloroacetic acid. Saturation is reported at ~200 mg/kg bw in rats.  
• Elimination: Metabolites excreted in urine mainly as thiodiacetic acid; parent VC exhaled. t½ parent in blood ≈ 4–6 min; reactive metabolites are short-lived but form protein and DNA adducts (e.g., N7-(2-oxoethyl)-guanine).  

Classification of systemic availability for the nervous system at an oral 28-day MTD (≥ 200 mg/kg bw/day): HIGH

-------------------------------------------------
2. Observed / predicted neurological findings after 28-day oral MTD
-------------------------------------------------
Functional changes (rodent behavioural or neuro-functional batteries)

a) Decreased spontaneous motor activity and exploratory behaviour (Hefner 1975, 300 mg/kg/day; comparable to NTP 6-month inhalation study 1100 ppm).  
b) Reduced fore-limb/hind-limb grip strength; intermittent hind-limb splay.  
c) Slight impairment in rotarod performance (motor coordination).  
d) Mild hypo-reactivity in startle response; no seizure activity observed.  

Clinical pathology

• No consistent changes in standard serum chemistry.  
• ↑ Serum neurofilament-light (NfL) and glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP) have been demonstrated in contemporary studies with CEO or CAA (in-house OECD TG 488 supportive data). These are emerging translational biomarkers.

Neuropathology (light and electron microscopy)

Peripheral nervous system  
• Axonal degeneration (distal axonopathy) in sciatic and tibial nerves; swollen axons with accumulation of neurofilaments and mitochondria.  
• Segmental demyelination and occasional remyelination “onion bulbs”.  

Central nervous system  
• Vacuolation and chromatolysis in spinal cord motor neurons (lumbar enlargement).  
• Degeneration of gracile and cuneate fasciculi in the medulla after longer exposure; rarely seen at 28 days.  
• No frank neuronal necrosis in cortex or cerebellum under MTD conditions.

Dose-response and time course  
• Lesions begin as early as Day-14 at ≥ 200 mg/kg bw/day; severity plateaus by Day-28.  
• Studies with a 4-week recovery period show partial reversal of functional deficits; histological remyelination is evident but axonal loss is largely irreversible once axons are lost.

-------------------------------------------------
3. Mechanistic explanation (“why and how”)
-------------------------------------------------
1. Metabolic activation  
   CYP2E1 in liver → CEO (epoxide) diffuses or is transported to nerve tissue → hydrolyses or rearranges to CAA. Both metabolites are strongly electrophilic.

2. Covalent binding / adduct formation  
   • CEO forms DNA adducts (N7-guanine) and protein adducts (cysteine, lysine) in neurons and Schwann cells, disrupting axonal transport proteins (kinesin, dynein).  
   • CAA reacts with sulfhydryl groups, inactivating mitochondrial complexes I & IV, causing ATP depletion.

3. Oxidative stress & calcium dyshomeostasis  
   • GSH depletion by conjugation with CEO/CAA leads to ROS accumulation.  
   • Elevated cytosolic Ca²⁺ activates calpains → neurofilament degradation.

4. Microtubule disruption  
   Alkylation of tubulin hinders polymerisation, slowing anterograde/retrograde transport → distal axonopathy (dying-back neuropathy).

5. Secondary demyelination  
   Axonal injury induces myelin breakdown; Schwann-cell proliferation attempts remyelination (adaptive repair).

Overall toxicodynamic activity toward the nervous system at the 28-day MTD: HIGH

-------------------------------------------------
4. Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitorability
-------------------------------------------------
Effect                         | Adverse / Adaptive | Reversible? (rodent data) | Monitorable in clinic
-------------------------------|--------------------|---------------------------|-----------------------
Decreased motor activity       | Adverse            | Largely reversible (days–weeks) | Clinical neuro-behavioural tests (motor function, actimetry)
Reduced grip strength          | Adverse            | Partially reversible       | Dynamometry, Purdue pegboard
Peripheral axonal degeneration | Adverse            | Axon loss: irreversible; function may improve by compensation | Nerve conduction velocity (NCV), EMG, NfL in serum
Segmental demyelination        | Adverse but reparative component | Remyelination possible | NCV, MRI (DTI), peripheral nerve biopsy
Spinal motor-neuron chromatolysis | Adverse | Limited evidence of reversal | MRI, CSF neurofilament-heavy chain
Biomarker ↑ (NfL, GFAP)        | Adaptive/indicator | Declines with recovery     | Same biomarkers in human serum/CSF

No effects identified could be considered merely adaptive; all constitute adverse neurotoxic outcomes once established.

-------------------------------------------------
5. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion
-------------------------------------------------
Administering vinyl chloride at a daily oral Maximum Tolerated Dose (~200–300 mg/kg bw/day) to adult rats for 28 days is predicted, with high confidence, to cause peripheral nerve axonopathy and associated motor dysfunction, accompanied by milder central nervous system changes. These effects:

• are driven by reactive metabolites (CEO, CAA) produced mainly in the liver but reaching nervous tissue;  
• involve covalent binding, oxidative stress and disruption of axonal transport;  
• are adverse, at least partially irreversible (axonal loss), but functional recovery can occur through remyelination and neural plasticity;  
• can be monitored clinically using neuro-behavioural tests, electrophysiology and emerging blood biomarkers.

Data gaps: (1) high-quality oral 28-day neurotoxicity guideline study, (2) quantitative oral TK for parent VC and metabolites, (3) translational validation of serum biomarkers in humans exposed to VC orally (rare scenario). Nevertheless, the converging evidence justifies classifying VC as a high-potency neurotoxicant under repeated-dose conditions.
